430	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
431	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
432	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
433	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
434	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
435	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
436	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
437	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
438	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
439	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
440	  normalisation with denominators).}
441	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
442	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
443	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
444	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
445	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
446	  \[
447	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
448	  \]
449	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
450	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
451	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
452	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
453	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
454	\end{lemma}
455	
456	\begin{proof}
457	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
458	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
459	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
460	  % NOTE: the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a Finset-fold over the finite
461	  % generating set sigma, but each fold step is the Mathlib collapsing lemma
462	  % IsIntegral.exists_multiple_integral_of_isLocalization applied to the generator AS A
463	  % WHOLE (not to its individual coefficient polynomials via gf_clear_one_denominator).
464	  % For each generator x, that lemma yields a single a_x in A^0 (nonzero, since A is a
465	  % domain) with C(a_x) * (1 (x) x) integral over MvPolynomial A; set g1 := prod_x a_x and
466	  % g := g0 * g1. The AlgHom assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
467	
468	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
469	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
470	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
471	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
472	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
473	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
474	
475	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
476	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
477	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
478	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
479	  \(x \in \sigma\) apply the Mathlib collapsing lemma
480	  \texttt{IsIntegral.exists\_multiple\_integral\_of\_isLocalization} (with
481	  \(R = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,A\), \(R_m = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,K\), \(S = B_K\), and the localizing
482	  submonoid the image of \(A^{0}\)): this returns a single
483	  \(a_x \in A^{0}\) (non-zero, as \(A\) is a domain) such that \(C(a_x)\cdot(1\otimes x)\)
484	  is integral over \(\mathrm{MvPoly}\,A = A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
485	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
486	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
487	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
488	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
489	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
490	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the landed proof discharges a whole generator
491	  at once via the collapsing lemma, which folds that primitive over the coefficients
492	  internally.)
493	
494	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
495	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
496	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
497	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
498	
499	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
500	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
501	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
502	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
503	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
504	  \[
505	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
506	  \]
507	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
508	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
509	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
510	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
511	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
512	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
513	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
514	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
515	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
516	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
517	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
518	
519	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
520	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
521	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
522	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
523	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
524	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
525	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
526	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
527	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
528	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
529	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
530	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
531	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
532	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
533	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
534	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
535	
536	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
537	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
538	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
539	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
540	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
541	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
542	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
543	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
544	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
545	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
546	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
547	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
548	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
549	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
550	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
551	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
552	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
553	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
554	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
555	\end{proof}
556	
557	\begin{lemma}[injectivity of a localisation lift]
558	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
559	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
560	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
561	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
562	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
563	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
564	\end{lemma}
565	\begin{proof}
566	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
567	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
568	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
569	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
570	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
571	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
572	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
573	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
574	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
575	\end{proof}
576	
577	\begin{lemma}
578	
579	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
580	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
581	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
582	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
583	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
584	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
585	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
586	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
587	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
588	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
589	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
590	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
591	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
592	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
593	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
594	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
595	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
596	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
597	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
598	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
599	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
600	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
601	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
602	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
603	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
604	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
605	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
606	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
607	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
608	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
609	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
610	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
611	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
612	  \[
613	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
614	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
615	  \]
616	
617	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
618	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
619	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
620	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
621	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
622	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
623	\end{lemma}
624	
625	\begin{proof}
626	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
627	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
628	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
629	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
630	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
631	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
632	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
633	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
634	
635	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
636	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
637	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
638	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
639	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
640	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
641	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
642	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
643	
644	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
645	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
646	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
647	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
648	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
649	\end{proof}

661	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
662	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
663	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
664	  \mathlibok
665	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
666	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
667	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
668	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
669	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
670	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
671	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
672	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
673	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
674	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
675	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
676	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
677	\end{lemma}

679	\begin{lemma}[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
680	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
681	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
682	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
683	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
684	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
685	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
686	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
687	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
688	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
689	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
690	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
691	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
692	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
693	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
694	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
695	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
696	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
697	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
698	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
699	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
700	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
701	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
702	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
703	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
704	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
705	\end{lemma}
706	
707	\begin{proof}
708	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
709	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
710	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
711	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
712	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
713	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
714	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
715	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
716	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
717	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
718	\end{proof}

720	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
721	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
722	
723	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
724	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
725	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
726	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
727	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
728	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
729	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
730	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
731	
732	\begin{definition}[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
733	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
734	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
735	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
736	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
737	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
738	  \[
739	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
740	  \]
741	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
742	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
743	\end{definition}
744	
745	\begin{lemma}[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
746	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
748	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
749	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
750	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
751	\end{lemma}
752	\begin{proof}
753	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
754	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
755	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\). Proved directly
756	  in Lean.
757	\end{proof}
758	
759	\begin{definition}[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
760	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
761	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
762	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
763	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
764	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
765	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
766	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
767	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
768	\end{definition}
769	
770	\begin{lemma}[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
771	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
772	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
773	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
774	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
775	\end{lemma}
776	\begin{proof}
777	  Proved directly in Lean.
778	\end{proof}
779	
780	\begin{lemma}[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
781	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
782	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
783	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
784	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
785	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
786	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
787	\end{lemma}
788	\begin{proof}
789	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
790	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
791	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
792	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
793	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
794	\end{proof}
795	
796	\begin{lemma}[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
797	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
798	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
799	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
800	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
801	  transformed monomial is the radix value
802	  \[
803	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
804	  \]
805	\end{lemma}
806	\begin{proof}
807	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
808	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
809	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
810	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
811	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
812	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
813	\end{proof}
814	
815	\begin{lemma}[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
816	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
817	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
818	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
819	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
820	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
821	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
822	\end{lemma}
823	\begin{proof}
824	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
825	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
826	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
827	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
828	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
829	\end{proof}

831	\begin{lemma}[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
832	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
833	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
834	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
835	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
836	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
837	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
838	\end{lemma}
839	\begin{proof}
840	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
841	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
842	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
843	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
844	\end{proof}

846	\begin{lemma}[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
847	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
848	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
849	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
850	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
851	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
852	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
853	\end{lemma}
854	\begin{proof}
855	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
856	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
857	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
858	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
859	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
860	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
861	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
862	\end{proof}

1243	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1244	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1245	% used in the codomain-read computation.
1246	
1247	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1248	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1249	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1250	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1251	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1252	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1253	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1254	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1255	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1256	\end{lemma}
1257	\begin{proof}
1258	
1259	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1260	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1261	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1262	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1263	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1264	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1265	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1266	\end{proof}
1267	
1268	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1269	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1270	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1271	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1272	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1273	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1274	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1275	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1276	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1277	\end{lemma}
1278	\begin{proof}
1279	
1280	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1281	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1282	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1283	\end{proof}

1285	% --- Section-level identity, three pieces: regroup equiv (pure tensor algebra, native
1286	%     R'-linear via Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange) ← section identity (Γ(θ) is the
1287	%     R'-base change of the algebraic unit) ← IsIso corollary (the pinned leaf). ---
1288	
1289	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1290	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1291	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1292	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1293	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1294	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1295	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1296	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1297	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1298	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1299	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1300	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1301	  % L933--938).
1302	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1303	  % equality
1304	  % $$
1305	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1306	  % $$
1307	  % as $R'$-modules."
1308	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1309	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1310	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1311	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1312	  \[
1313	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1314	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1315	  \]
1316	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1317	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1318	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1319	  hypothesis.
1320	\end{lemma}
1321	
1322	\begin{proof}
1323	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1324	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1325	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the standalone geometry-free helper
1326	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}, whose construction is now the
1327	  natively \(R'\)-linear pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1328	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1329	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1330	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1331	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1332	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1333	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1334	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1335	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1336	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1337	
1338	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1339	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1340	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1341	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1342	  dictionaries,
1343	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1344	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1345	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1346	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1347	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1348	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1349	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1350	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1351	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1352	  \[
1353	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1354	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1355	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1356	  \]
1357	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1358	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1359	
1360	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1361	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1362	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1363	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1364	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1365	\end{proof}

1371	%       (3) lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose — the (g^* ⊣ g_*) transpose on sections.

1396	  %       ((moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := A)).mapIso

1398	  %               (pullback_spec_tilde_iso inclA M) ≪≫
1399	  %             pushforward_spec_tilde_iso inclA ((ModuleCat.extendScalars inclA.hom).obj M))
1400	  %         ≪≫ (tilde.toTildeΓNatIso.app
1401	  %               ((ModuleCat.restrictScalars inclA.hom).obj
1402	  %                 ((ModuleCat.extendScalars inclA.hom).obj M))).symm).hom
1403	  %       = (ModuleCat.extendRestrictScalarsAdj inclA.hom).unit.app M
1404	  Let \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\) be the canonical inclusion
1405	  (\(a \mapsto a \otimes 1\)). The unit of the
1406	  \(\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^*, (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\bigr)\)-adjunction
1407	  evaluated at the affine sheaf \(\widetilde M\), read on the global sections over
1408	  \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) through the tilde dictionaries

1410	  the tilde--\(\Gamma\) unit \(\widetilde{(-)}\Gamma\)-iso, equals the algebraic unit of the
1411	  extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\iota_A\), namely
1412	  \(\eta_M : M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\), \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\).

1416	  This is the affine, square-free heart of the computation: there is no pullback square and
1417	  no base-change map yet, only the comparison of two units of two adjunctions related by the

1425	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\). The dictionaries being the comparison isomorphisms of these

1457	\begin{lemma}[Unit-across-conjugate coherence]
1458	  \label{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}
1459	  \lean{CategoryTheory.unit_conjugateEquiv}
1460	  \mathlibok
1461	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1462	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\) and \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\) be two
1463	  adjunctions between the same pair of categories, with units \(\eta_1\) and \(\eta_2\), and let
1464	  \(\alpha : L_2 \Rightarrow L_1\) be a natural transformation of the left adjoints. Write
1465	  \(\operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2)\) for the conjugation (mate) bijection,
1466	  which carries \(\alpha\) to a natural transformation \(R_1 \Rightarrow R_2\) of the right
1467	  adjoints. Then for every object \(c\),
1468	  \[
1469	    (\eta_1)_c \circ \bigl(\operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2)\,\alpha\bigr)_{L_1 c}
1470	      \;=\; (\eta_2)_c \circ R_2(\alpha_c) ,
1471	  \]
1472	  the transposed form of the conjugation relating the two units across the mate of \(\alpha\).
1473	\end{lemma}

1475	\begin{lemma}[Unit of a composite adjunction]

1490	\begin{lemma}[The pullback--pushforward conjugate sends the pullback coherence to the pushforward coherence]
1491	  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1492	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv}
1493	  \mathlibok
1494	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1495	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) and \(g : Y \to Z\) be composable morphisms of schemes, and write
1496	  \((h^{*} \dashv h_{*})\) for the pullback--pushforward adjunction on \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
1497	  attached to a morphism \(h\). Under the conjugation (mate) bijection between the composite
1498	  adjunction \((g^{*}\dashv g_{*}) . (f^{*}\dashv f_{*})\) and the adjunction
1499	  \(\bigl((f\circ g)^{*} \dashv (f\circ g)_{*}\bigr)\), the inverse pullback-composition coherence

1510	\begin{lemma}
1511	
1512	\leanok
1513	[Pseudofunctoriality of the pullback--pushforward unit]
1514	  \label{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1516	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1517	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1518	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to X_3\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
1519	  \(N\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_3}\)-module. Write \(\eta^{h}\) for the unit of the
1520	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \((h^{*}\dashv h_{*})\) attached to a morphism \(h\), and
1521	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\), \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b}\) for the pseudofunctor
1522	  composition coherences. Then
1523	  \[
1524	    \eta^{b}_{N} \;\circ\; b_{*}\bigl(\eta^{a}_{\,b^{*}N}\bigr)
1525	      \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{N}
1526	    \;=\;
1527	    \eta^{a\circ b}_{N} \;\circ\;
1528	      (a\circ b)_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_{N}\bigr),
1529	  \]
1530	  where composites are read left to right. In words: the unit of the composite morphism
1531	  \(a \circ b\), reindexed by the inverse pullback coherence, factors as the \(b\)-unit, followed
1532	  by \(b_{*}\) of the \(a\)-unit, followed by the pushforward coherence. This is the leg-reindex
1533	  engine consumed by Seam~2 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}): with \(a\) an
1534	  isomorphism and \(b\) an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map it turns the generic projection-leg
1535	  unit into the affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-unit modulo the transparent coherences.
1536	\end{lemma}
1537	\begin{proof}
1538	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1539	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1540	  Apply the unit-across-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib} to the
1541	  composite adjunction \((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}) . (a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\), the adjunction
1542	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1543	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1544	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by
1545	  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} that mate is exactly the pushforward
1546	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}\). Expanding the unit of the composite
1547	  adjunction by Lemma~\ref{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib} —
1548	  \(\bigl((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}).(a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\bigr)\text{-unit}_N
1549	    = \eta^{b}_N \circ b_{*}(\eta^{a}_{b^{*}N})\) — and reassociating the resulting composite gives
1550	  the stated equation.
1551	\end{proof}

1601	\begin{lemma}
1602	
1603	\leanok
1604	[$\Gamma$-collapse of the pushforward-congruence coherence]
1605	  \label{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardCongr_hom}
1606	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaMap_pushforwardCongr_hom}
1607	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
1608	  Let \(f, g : X \to \operatorname{Spec} R\) be morphisms of schemes with \(f = g\), and \(M\)
1609	  an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Applying the global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) to the component

1645	  Let \(e : X' \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Spec}(A \otimes_R R')\) be the canonical
1646	  isomorphism, \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\) the left tensor inclusion, and set
1647	  \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\). For an \(A\)-module \(M\), the bare \((g')\)-unit
1648	  \(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\) of the \((g')\)-adjunction expands as the four-factor composite
1649	  \[
1650	    \eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}
1651	      \;=\;
1652	      \eta^{\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}_{\widetilde M}
1653	      \,\circ\, (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}\bigl(\eta^{e}_{(\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^{*}\widetilde M}\bigr)
1654	      \,\circ\, (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{\widetilde M}
1655	      \,\circ\, (g')_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{\widetilde M}\bigr),
1656	  \]
1657	  where composites are read left to right.
1658	\end{lemma}
1659	\begin{proof}
1660	  \uses{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1661	  The leg-reindex engine Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}, instantiated at \(a = e\),
1662	  \(b = \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), \(N = \widetilde M\), reads
1663	  \[
1664	    \eta^{\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}_{\widetilde M}
1665	      \circ (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}(\eta^{e})
1666	      \circ (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}
1667	    \;=\;
1668	    \eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}
1669	      \circ (g')_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{inv}}\bigr).
1670	  \]
1671	  Post-compose both sides with \((g')_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}\bigr)\).
1672	  Since \((g')_{*}\) is a functor it carries
1673	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp})^{\mathrm{inv}} \circ (\mathrm{pullbackComp})^{\mathrm{hom}} = \mathrm{id}\)
1674	  to the identity, so the right-hand side collapses to \(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\), giving the stated
1675	  expansion.
1676	\end{proof}

887	\begin{lemma}[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
888	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
889	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
890	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
891	  % NOTE (variable convention): the project's univariate elimination uses
892	  %   MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv, which singles out variable X_0 as the polynomial
893	  %   variable over the coefficient ring MvPolynomial (Fin n) A. The choice of which of
894	  %   the d = n+1 variables is "distinguished/eliminated" is immaterial (a renaming);
895	  %   the informal prose elsewhere says "last variable X_d" but the Lean uses X_0.
896	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
897	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
898	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
899	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
900	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
901	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
902	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
903	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
904	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g; map := MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A A_g).
905	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
906	  %       (F : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A) (hF : F ≠ 0) :
907	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
908	  %         (e : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A ≃ₐ[A] MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A),
909	  %         IsUnit
910	  %           (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv (Localization.Away g) n
911	  %             (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A (Localization.Away g)) (e F))).leadingCoeff
912	  %   The automorphism e is the triangular Nagata substitution; g is the (non-zero)
913	  %   leading coefficient that becomes a unit after inversion.
914	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
915	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
916	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
917	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
918	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
919	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
920	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
921	\end{lemma}
922	
923	\begin{proof}
924	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
925	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
926	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
927	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
928	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
929	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
930	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
931	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
932	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
933	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
934	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
935	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
936	\end{proof}

938	\begin{lemma}[L5b engine anchor — quotient by a monic univariate polynomial is finite]
939	  \label{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite}
940	  \lean{Polynomial.Monic.finite_quotient}
941	  \mathlibok
942	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
943	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(f \in S[X]\) a monic polynomial. Then the quotient
944	  \(S[X]/(f)\) is a module-finite \(S\)-module (indeed free of rank \(\deg f\)).
945	\end{lemma}

947	\begin{lemma}[L5b.3 — single-variable elimination engine (shared)]
948	  \label{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic}
949	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_of_monic_lastVar}
950	  \uses{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
951	  % NOTE (F-3a): the LEAN SIGNATURE below now matches the landed
952	  % decl, which encodes finiteness as `RingHom.Finite` of the composite ring map
953	  % (the earlier `Module.Finite` + `letI : Algebra …` sketch was equivalent math but a
954	  % different formal type). A caller (`gf_torsion_reindex`) must `apply` this lemma and
955	  % discharge a `RingHom.Finite` goal, NOT a `Module.Finite` goal.
956	  % NOTE (shared engine): this is the standalone division-algorithm primitive that both
957	  % L5b (lem:gf_torsion_reindex, after inverting the Nagata leading coefficient) and L4
958	  % (lem:gf_clear_one_denominator / lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, after clearing
959	  % denominators) consume. It is stated over a GENERAL base ring R so it is not tied to
960	  % the torsion module T; build it once in Lean and reuse for both. The hypothesis
961	  % "leading coefficient is a unit" (monic up to a unit) is what the inversion step in
962	  % each caller produces; the strict-monic Mathlib anchor is reached inside the proof by
963	  % rescaling by the unit.
964	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
965	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
966	  % the division algorithm in one variable is the standard mechanism of the support drop.
967	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
968	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
969	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
970	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
971	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (matches the landed decl verbatim).
972	  %   Finiteness is the `RingHom.Finite` of the composite ring map
973	  %   R[X_1,…,X_n] ↪ R[X_0,…,X_n] ↠ R[X_0,…,X_n]/(p)
974	  %   (constant inclusion X_j ↦ X_{j+1} = MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ, then
975	  %   Ideal.Quotient.mk). A caller discharges a `RingHom.Finite` goal, not `Module.Finite`.
976	  %     (R : Type*) [CommRing R] (n : ℕ)
977	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R)
978	  %       (hp : IsUnit (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p).leadingCoeff) :
979	  %       ((Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {p})).comp
980	  %           (MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ).toRingHom :
981	  %             MvPolynomial (Fin n) R →+* (MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R ⧸ Ideal.span {p})).Finite
982	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (single-variable elimination).}
983	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and \(p \in R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) a
984	  polynomial whose image under the isomorphism
985	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) has a \emph{unit} leading
986	  coefficient — i.e.\ \(p\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to a unit. Then the
987	  quotient \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
988	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) (embedded by \(X_j \mapsto X_j\), \(j = 1, \dots, n\)).
989	\end{lemma}
990	
991	\begin{proof}
992	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
993	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (single-variable elimination).}
994	  Write \(S := R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) and let
995	  \(\Phi : R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S[X_0]\) be the standard \(R\)-algebra
996	  isomorphism, which sends \(X_0\) to the univariate variable and embeds \(S\) as the constants. Put \(q := \Phi(p) \in S[X_0]\); by hypothesis its leading
997	  coefficient \(u\) is a unit, so the associate \(\tilde q := u^{-1} \cdot q\) is monic and
998	  \((\tilde q) = (q)\) in \(S[X_0]\). The isomorphism \(\Phi\) carries the ideal \((p)\) onto
999	  \((q) = (\tilde q)\), hence descends to a ring isomorphism
1000	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p) \cong S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) that is \(S\)-linear, because \(S\) sits
1001	  inside both sides as the constants (respectively as the coefficient subring
1002	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)). By \cref{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite} the quotient
1003	  \(S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) is module-finite over \(S\); transporting finiteness along the
1004	  \(S\)-linear isomorphism shows \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over
1005	  \(S = R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
1006	\end{proof}

1010	[L5b — torsion reindex onto fewer variables]
1011	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}
1012	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_reindex}
1013	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
1014	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1015	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1016	  % NOTE (shared engine): the single-variable Nagata change-of-variables together with
1017	  % the denominator-clearing after inverting one element of A is the SAME engine the
1018	  % Noether-normalisation step L4 (lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, via its sub-lemma
1019	  % lem:gf_clear_one_denominator) requires. It should be built once in Lean and reused by
1020	  % both: a general "make a nonzero F monic in the last variable and clear the leading
1021	  % coefficient into one Localization.Away g" helper discharges this lemma and feeds L4.
1022	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1023	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768).
1024	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1025	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
1026	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
1027	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
1028	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
1029	  %   Write A_g := Localization.Away g and T_g := LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T.
1030	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
1031	  %       (d : ℕ) (hd : 0 < d) (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T]
1032	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1033	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1034	  %       [Module A T] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1035	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
1036	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0) (m' : ℕ) (_ : m' < d)
1037	  %         (_ : Module (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1038	  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T))
1039	  %         -- NOTE: the redundant canonical `Module A_g T_g` existential was
1040	  %         -- dropped from the landed decl (it is always `inferInstance`; bundling it created an
1041	  %         -- opaque fvar breaking OreLocalization instance matching at the L5 use-site).
1042	  %         (_ : IsScalarTower (Localization.Away g)
1043	  %                 (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1044	  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)),
1045	  %         Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1046	  %           (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)
1047	  %   one may always take m' = d - 1 (single-variable elimination); the strong induction
1048	  %   accepts any m' < d.
1049	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
1050	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
1051	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
1052	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
1053	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1054	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
1055	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
1056	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
1057	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
1058	\end{lemma}
1059	
1060	\begin{proof}
1061	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
1062	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
1063	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
1064	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1065	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1066	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
1067	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
1068	
1069	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
1070	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
1071	
1072	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
1073	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
1074	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
1075	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
1076	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
1077	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
1078	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
1079	
1080	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
1081	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
1082	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
1083	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
1084	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
1085	  \(A_g\).
1086	
1087	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
1088	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
1089	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
1090	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
1091	
1092	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
1093	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
1094	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
1095	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
1096	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
1097	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
1098	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
1099	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
1100	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
1101	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
1102	
1103	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
1104	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
1105	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
1106	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
1107	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
1108	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
1109	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
1110	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
1111	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
1112	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
1113	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
1114	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
1115	
1116	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
1117	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
1118	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
1119	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
1120	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
1121	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
1122	
1123	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
1124	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
1125	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
1126	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
1127	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
1128	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
1129	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
1130	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
1131	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
1132	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
1133	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
1134	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
1135	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
1136	
1137	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
1138	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
1139	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1140	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1141	\end{proof}

1143	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
1144	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
1145	
1146	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1147	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1148	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1149	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1150	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
1151	
1152	\begin{lemma}[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1153	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1154	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1155	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1156	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1157	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1158	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1159	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1160	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1161	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1162	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1163	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1164	\end{lemma}
1165	\begin{proof}
1166	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1167	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1168	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1169	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1170	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1171	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1172	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1173	\end{proof}
1174	
1175	\begin{lemma}[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1176	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1177	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1178	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1179	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1180	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1181	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1182	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1183	\end{lemma}
1184	\begin{proof}
1185	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1186	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1187	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1188	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1189	\end{proof}
1190	
1191	\begin{lemma}[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1192	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1194	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1195	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1196	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1197	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1198	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1199	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1200	\end{lemma}
1201	\begin{proof}
1202	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1203	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1204	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1205	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1206	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1207	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1208	  these conditions gives the claim.
1209	\end{proof}

1211	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1212	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1213	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1214	  \mathlibok
1215	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1216	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1217	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1218	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1219	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1220	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1221	\end{lemma}

1223	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1224	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1225	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1226	  \mathlibok
1227	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1228	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1229	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1230	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1231	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).
1232	\end{lemma}

1234	\begin{lemma}[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1235	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1236	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1237	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1238	  % LEAN DEPS: the localisation-transport primitives the Lean proof consumes are
1239	  %   IsLocalization.surj                          (clear the denominator of h over A_g)
1240	  %   IsLocalization.map_units                     (the cleared denominator s ∈ powers g is a unit)
1241	  %   IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated        (key ring identification: Away(g·a) on A_h)
1242	  %   IsLocalization.algEquiv                      (ring iso Away(g·a) ≅ A_h)
1243	  %   Module.Free.of_ringEquiv                     (transport freeness across that ring iso)
1244	  %   IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv                (the σ-semilinear module equivalence)
1245	  %   LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization    (upgrade the R-linear equiv to Away(g·a)-linear)
1246	  % The one primitive NOT packaged by Mathlib — that the composite localisation map
1247	  %   ψ := mkLinearMap(powers h) ∘ mkLinearMap(powers g) : T → (T_g)_h
1248	  % satisfies IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ — is proved directly from the three
1249	  % IsLocalizedModule axioms (units act invertibly, surjectivity up to denominators, the
1250	  % equality criterion); there is no packaged localisation-of-localisation lemma to invoke.
1251	  % NOTE: the closed Lean proof DID find a packaged route — the composite-localisation
1252	  % `IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ` step goes through `IsBaseChange.comp` via
1253	  % `isLocalizedModule_iff_isBaseChange`, so the "no packaged lemma" remark above is superseded.
1254	  % WITNESS: the conclusion holds at the SINGLE product f := g·a (not at a square f²).
1255	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1256	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1257	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1258	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1259	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1260	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1261	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1262	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1263	\end{lemma}
1264	
1265	\begin{proof}
1266	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1267	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1268	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1269	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1270	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1271	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1272	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1273	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1274	
1275	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1276	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1277	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1278	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1279	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1280	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1281	
1282	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1283	  \[
1284	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1285	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1286	  \]
1287	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1288	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1289	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1290	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1291	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1292	
1293	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1294	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1295	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1296	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1297	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1298	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1299	\end{proof}
