1301	\begin{lemma}[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1302	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1304	  % NOTE (signature): the Lean decl uses a SHARED universe
1305	  %   `(A N : Type u)` (not `(A : Type*) (N : Type*)`). This is load-bearing, not a
1306	  %   convenience: the base-domain-generalizing strong induction applies its IH at
1307	  %   `A_g = Localization.Away g`, and `LocalizedModule S M : Type (max u_R u_M)`, so the
1308	  %   reindexed module escapes `N`'s universe unless `A` and `N` share one. The geometric
1309	  %   target lives entirely in `Type u` (`Scheme.{u}`), so this is the natural setting.
1310	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1311	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1312	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1313	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1314	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1315	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1316	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1317	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1318	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1319	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1320	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1321	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1322	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1323	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1324	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1325	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1326	  core of the induction).}
1327	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1328	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1329	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1330	
1331	\end{lemma}
1332	
1333	\begin{proof}
1334	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1335	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1336	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1337	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1338	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1339	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE: the induction must generalise the base domain A
1340	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1341	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1342	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1343	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
1344	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
1345	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
1346	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
1347	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
1348	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1349	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1350	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1351	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1352	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1353	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1354	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1355	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1356	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1357	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1358	
1359	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1360	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1361	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1362	
1363	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1364	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1365	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1366	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1367	  \begin{enumerate}
1368	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1369	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1370	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1371	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1372	      \(P_d\)-modules
1373	      \[
1374	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1375	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1376	      \]
1377	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1378	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1379	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1380	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1381	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1382	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1383	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1384	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1385	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1386	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1387	      to \(A_g\).
1388	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1389	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1390	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1391	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1392	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1393	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1394	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1395	      over \(A_f\).
1396	      % NOTE: the tower-descent helper lem:gf_away_tower_descent
1397	      % (free_localizationAway_of_away_tower) is now CLOSED and axiom-clean. The L5 sorry that
1398	      % remains is NOT a missing math step — it is a Lean elaboration blocker: the
1399	      % `OreLocalization` instance *presentations* on the localised quotient `(N ⧸ range φ)_g`
1400	      % (CommSemiring / AddCommMonoid / Module-SMul) emitted by `gf_torsion_reindex` are defeq
1401	      % but not instance-transparent-equal to the presentations the helper input expects. The
1402	      % clean fix is non-local: make `gf_torsion_reindex` emit its conclusion over the canonical
1403	      % `OreLocalization.*` instances (its final `exact` / existential binders), after which the
1404	      % recorded five-line assembly closes verbatim. Do NOT re-dispatch a raw L5 prover round
1405	      % without that instance-alignment refactor first.
1406	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1407	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1408	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1409	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1410	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1411	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1412	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1413	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1414	      completes the induction.
1415	  \end{enumerate}
1416	\end{proof}

1418	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
1419	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
1420	
1421	The following two lemmas package the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms
1422	(flatness and module-finiteness) used by the dévissage chain.
1423	
1424	\begin{lemma}
1425	
1426	
1427	[Flat localization away, finite module]
1428	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
1429	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
1430	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1431	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
1432	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is flat over \(A_f\).
1433	\end{lemma}
1434	\begin{proof}
1435	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1436	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); free modules are flat.
1437	\end{proof}
1438	
1439	\begin{lemma}
1440	
1441	
1442	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
1443	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
1444	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
1445	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1446	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a
1447	  finite \(B\)-module with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower
1448	  \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\)
1449	  is free over \(A_f\).
1450	\end{lemma}
1451	\begin{proof}
1452	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1453	  Since \(B\) is module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\),
1454	  transitivity of module-finiteness makes \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module.
1455	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} then gives the required \(f\).
1456	\end{proof}

1459	\begin{theorem}
1460	
1461	
1462	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1463	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1464	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1465	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1466	  % NOTE (re-signed): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
1467	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
1468	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any
1469	  % non-empty open). The decl (not in archon-protected.yaml) was re-signed to carry
1470	  % the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is the source of truth for the exact
1471	  % binders and the full flatness conclusion.
1472	  %
1473	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
1474	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
1475	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
1476	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
1477	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
1478	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
1479	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
1480	  %
1481	  % LEAN SIGNATURE HEADER (now landed in the .lean file):
1482	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
1483	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] (F : X.Modules)
1484	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
1485	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
1486	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
1487	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
1488	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
1489	
1490	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1491	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1492	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1493	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1494	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1495	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1496	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1497	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1498	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1499	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1500	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1501	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1502	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1503	
1504	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1505	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1506	\end{theorem}
1507	
1508	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1509	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1510	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1511	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1512	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1513	% by restricting to a non-empty
1514	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1515	\begin{proof}
1516	  
1517	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1518	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1519	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1520	
1521	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
1522	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
1523	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
1524	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
1525	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1526	
1527	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1528	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1529	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-type hypothesis makes \(M_j\)
1530	  a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is
1531	  the data fed to the algebraic form.
1532	
1533	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1534	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1535	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1536	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1537	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1538	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1539	
1540	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1541	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the fibrewise flatness conclusion then follows from
1542	  these local freeness statements by the criterion that a module flat at every maximal
1543	  ideal is flat. This produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1544	\end{proof}

1546	\section{Mathlib status}
1547	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1548	
1549	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1550	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1551	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1552	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1553	generic-flatness argument consumes
1554	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1555	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1556	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1557	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1558	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1559	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1560	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1561	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1562	are introduced in
1563	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.

2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Left Cramer inverse]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Right Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
104	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
120	
121	\begin{definition}
122	
123	\leanok
124	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
125	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
127	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
129	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
133	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
134	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
135	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
136	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). With the chart ring
137	  %   `R^I := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ I}) ℤ`, define
138	  %   `universalMatrix d r I : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) R^I` whose entry at
139	  %   `(p, q)` is: the identity entry `if (orderIso I) p = q then 1 else 0`
140	  %   when `q ∈ I` (using the order isomorphism `Fin d ≃o I` from `#I = d`),
141	  %   and the indeterminate `MvPolynomial.X (p, ⟨q, hq⟩)` when `q ∉ I`.
142	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
143	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
144	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
145	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
146	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
147	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
148	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
149	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
150	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
151	\end{definition}
152	
153	\begin{definition}
154	
155	\leanok
156	[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
157	  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
159	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
160	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
161	  % affine patches" (read from
162	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
163	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
164	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
165	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
166	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
167	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). `minorDet d r I J : R^I` is the
168	  %   determinant of the `d × d` submatrix of `universalMatrix d r I` whose
169	  %   columns are indexed by `J`, reindexed to `Fin d` via the order
170	  %   isomorphism `Fin d ≃o J` coming from `#J = d`:
171	  %   `(universalMatrix d r I).submatrix id (orderIso J) |>.det`.
172	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
173	  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
174	  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
175	  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
176	  \[
177	    P^I_J \;:=\; \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
178	  \]
179	  Then \(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (the localisation of the chart ring
180	  away from \(P^I_J\)) is the ring of the principal open subscheme
181	  \(U^I_J = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J] \subseteq U^I\) on which \(P^I_J\) is
182	  invertible. One has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\), so \(R^I_I =
183	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) and \(U^I_I = U^I\).
184	\end{definition}
185	
186	\begin{definition}
187	
188	\leanok
189	[The localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\) over \(R^I_J\)]
190	  \label{def:gr_universal_minor}
191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinor}
192	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det}
193	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
194	  % affine patches" (read from
195	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
196	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
197	  % "Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
198	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible."
199	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
200	  %   `universalMinor d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
201	  %   is the `J`-minor of `universalMatrix d r I` base-changed along the
202	  %   structure map `R^I → R^I_J = Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`:
203	  %   `((universalMatrix d r I).submatrix id (orderIso J)).map (algebraMap _ _)`.
204	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
205	  Write \(X^I_J \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) for the \(J\)-minor of
206	  the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) with its entries
207	  pushed forward along the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\) into the
208	  localised chart ring \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\)
209	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}). By construction \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of
210	  \(P^I_J\) in \(R^I_J\).
211	\end{definition}
212	
213	\begin{lemma}
214	
215	\leanok
216	[The localised minor determinant is a unit]
217	  \label{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
218	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_det_universalMinor}
219	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
220	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
221	  % affine patches" (read from
222	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
223	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
224	  % "the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
225	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
226	  %  on $U^I_J$."
227	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
228	  %   `isUnit_det_universalMinor d r I J : IsUnit (universalMinor d r I J).det`.
229	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
230	  The determinant of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
231	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}) is a unit of \(R^I_J\):
232	  \[
233	    \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \in (R^I_J)^{\times}.
234	  \]
235	\end{lemma}
236	\begin{proof}
237	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
238	  \leanok
239	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_minor}, \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of \(P^I_J\)
240	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) under the structure map
241	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\). Since \(R^I_J\) is the localisation away from
242	  \(P^I_J\), that image is a unit by
243	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}.
244	\end{proof}

246	\begin{definition}
247	
248	\leanok
249	[The Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\)]
250	  \label{def:gr_universalMinorInv}
251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv}
252	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor}
253	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
254	  % affine patches" (read from

256	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
257	  % "the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$

260	  %   `universalMinorInv d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`

262	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
263	  Let \((X^I_J)^{-1} \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) be the
264	  nonsingular (Cramer) inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
265	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}). Its entries lie in \(R^I_J\) by Cramer's rule.
266	\end{definition}
267	
268	\begin{lemma}
269	
270	\leanok
271	[The Cramer inverse is a two-sided inverse]
272	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_mul_cancel}
274	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
275	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). A conjunction (or pair of
276	  %   lemmas):
277	  %   `universalMinorInv d r I J * universalMinor d r I J = 1` and
278	  %   `universalMinor d r I J * universalMinorInv d r I J = 1`.
279	  Because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}),
280	  the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) is a
281	  genuine two-sided inverse:
282	  \[
283	    (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}
284	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
285	    X^I_J\, (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}.
286	  \]
287	\end{lemma}

292	  \cref{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul} (left identity) and
293	  \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv} (right identity), both of which apply once
294	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is known to be a unit.
295	\end{proof}

351	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism out of the chart ring of \(J\) determined,
352	  via the universal property of the polynomial ring, by sending each free
353	  indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the image matrix \(M =

359	\begin{lemma}
360	
361	\leanok
362	[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
363	  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}
364	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
365	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
366	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
367	  % affine patches" (read from
368	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
370	  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
371	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
372	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
373	  %   `isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J :`
374	  %   `IsUnit (transitionPreMap d r I J (minorDet d r J I))`.
375	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
376	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
377	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
378	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
379	  \[
380	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
381	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
382	  \]
383	  which is a unit.
384	\end{lemma}
385	\begin{proof}
386	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
387	  \leanok
388	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
389	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
390	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
391	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
392	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
393	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
394	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
395	\end{proof}
396	
397	\begin{definition}
398	
399	\leanok
400	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
401	  \label{def:gr_transition}
402	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
403	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
404	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
405	  % affine patches" (read from
406	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
407	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
408	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
409	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
410	  %  defined as follows. ...
411	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
412	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
413	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). The away-localisation lift of the
414	  %   pre-hom along `P^J_I`:
415	  %   `transitionMap d r I J :`
416	  %   `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I) →+* Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
417	  %   `:= IsLocalization.Away.lift (minorDet d r J I)`
418	  %   `(isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J)` applied to
419	  %   `(transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →+* R^I_J)`.
420	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
421	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
422	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
423	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
424	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
425	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
426	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
427	  \[
428	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
429	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
430	  \]
431	  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
432	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
433	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
434	\end{definition}
435	
436	\begin{lemma}
437	
438	\leanok
439	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
440	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
442	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
443	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
444	  % affine patches" (read from
445	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
446	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
447	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
448	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
449	  %   `transitionMap_self d r I : transitionMap d r I I = RingHom.id _`
450	  %   (up to the identification `R^I_I = R^I` from `P^I_I = 1`).
451	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
452	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
453	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
454	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
455	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
456	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
457	\end{lemma}
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
460	  \leanok
461	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
462	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
463	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
464	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
465	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
466	\end{proof}

468	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
469	
470	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
471	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
472	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[Column submatrix of a product]
478	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
480	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
481	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
482	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
483	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
484	  the right factor:
485	  \[
486	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
487	  \]
488	\end{lemma}
489	\begin{proof}
490	
491	\leanok
492	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
493	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
494	\end{proof}
495	
496	\begin{lemma}
497	
498	\leanok
499	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
500	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
502	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
503	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
504	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
505	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
506	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
507	  \[
508	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
509	  \]
510	\end{lemma}
511	\begin{proof}
512	
513	\leanok
514	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
515	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
516	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
517	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
518	\end{proof}
519	
520	\begin{lemma}
521	
522	\leanok
523	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
524	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
526	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
527	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
528	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
529	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
530	\end{lemma}
531	\begin{proof}
532	
533	\leanok
534	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
535	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
536	\end{proof}
537	
538	\begin{lemma}
539	
540	\leanok
541	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
542	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
544	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
545	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
546	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
547	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
548	  \[
549	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
550	  \]
551	\end{lemma}
552	\begin{proof}
553	
554	\leanok
555	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
556	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
557	\end{proof}
558	
559	\begin{lemma}
560	
561	\leanok
562	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
563	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
564	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
565	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
566	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
567	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
568	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
569	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
570	  defining the chart.)
571	\end{lemma}
572	\begin{proof}
573	
574	\leanok
575	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
576	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the preimage of \(q\) under the
577	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\); so the \(I\)-minor
578	  is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
579	\end{proof}
580	
581	\begin{lemma}
582	
583	\leanok
584	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
585	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
586	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
587	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
588	  The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
589	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the identity:
590	  \[
591	    M_J \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J \;=\; 1_{d\times d}.
592	  \]
593	\end{lemma}
594	\begin{proof}
595	
596	\leanok
597	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
598	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
599	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
600	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
601	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
602	\end{proof}
603	
604	\begin{lemma}
605	
606	\leanok
607	[The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix is the Cramer inverse]
608	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
610	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
611	  The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
612	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the Cramer inverse:
613	  \[
614	    M_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}.
615	  \]
616	\end{lemma}
617	\begin{proof}
618	
619	\leanok
620	  By \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col} the \(I\)-column submatrix of \(M\) is
621	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\), and \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\)
622	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
623	\end{proof}
624	
625	\begin{lemma}
626	
627	\leanok
628	[\(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) realises the matrix formula]
629	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
630	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
631	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
632	  Applying the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
633	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) entrywise to the universal matrix \(X^J\) yields
634	  the image matrix:
635	  \[
636	    (\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast\, X^J \;=\; M \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
637	  \]
638	\end{lemma}
639	\begin{proof}
640	
641	\leanok
642	  On a free entry \(x^J_{p,q}\) (\(q \notin J\)) this holds by the definition of
643	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) as evaluation at the entries of \(M\)
644	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}). On a column \(q \in J\) the entry of \(X^J\) is
645	  an identity-block entry, which \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries to the
646	  corresponding entry of \(M_J = 1_{d\times d}\)
647	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
648	\end{proof}
649	
650	\begin{lemma}
651	
652	\leanok
653	[Image of a cross minor under \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)]
654	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
655	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet}
656	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
657	  For a third size-\(d\) subset \(K\), the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) sends the
658	  minor determinant \(P^J_K\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) to the determinant of the
659	  \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\):
660	  \[
661	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr) \;=\; \det\bigl(M_K\bigr).
662	  \]
663	\end{lemma}
664	\begin{proof}
665	
666	\leanok
667	  The hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) commutes with determinants and with passing to
668	  a column submatrix, so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
669	  \det\bigl((\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast X^J\bigr)_K\); apply
670	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}. Proved directly in Lean.
671	\end{proof}
672	
673	\begin{lemma}
674	
675	\leanok
676	[Naturality of the image matrix]
677	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}
678	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_eq}
679	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_universal_minor}
680	  Let \(X\) be a size-\(d\) subset and \(\iota : R^I_X \to D\) a ring
681	  homomorphism lying over the structure map \(R^I \to D\), i.e.\
682	  \(\iota \circ (R^I \to R^I_X) = (R^I \to D)\). Writing \(Y\) for the universal
683	  matrix \(X^I\) base-changed to \(D\) and \(Y_X\) for its \(X\)-minor, one has
684	  \[
685	    \iota_\ast\bigl((X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\bigr) \;=\; (Y_X)^{-1}\, Y .
686	  \]
687	\end{lemma}
688	\begin{proof}
689	
690	\leanok
691	  Distribute \(\iota_\ast\) across the product \(M = (X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\); the
692	  factor \((X^I_X)^{-1}\) passes through \(\iota_\ast\) as a Cramer inverse
693	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}, using \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), and both
694	  factors collapse to base changes over \(D\) by functoriality
695	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}) since \(\iota\) lies over \(R^I \to D\).
696	  Proved directly in Lean.
697	\end{proof}
698	
699	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
700	
701	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
702	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
703	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
704	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
705	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
706	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
707	\[
708	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
709	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
710	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
711	\]
712	
713	\begin{definition}
714	
715	\leanok
716	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
717	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}
718	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft}
719	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
720	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
721	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
722	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
723	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
724	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
725	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
726	\end{definition}
727	
728	\begin{definition}
729	
730	\leanok
731	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (right)]
732	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_right}
733	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight}
734	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
735	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
736	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} : R[1/y] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
737	  \(x\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
738	  \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(y\) — well-defined since \(y\) is a unit of
739	  \(R[1/(xy)]\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
740	\end{definition}
741	
742	\begin{lemma}
743	
744	\leanok
745	[Left inclusion lies over \(R\)]
746	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
748	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_left}
749	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left})
750	  intertwines the two structure maps:
751	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/x]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
752	\end{lemma}
753	\begin{proof}
754	
755	\leanok
756	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
757	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
758	\end{proof}
759	
760	\begin{lemma}
761	
762	\leanok
763	[Right inclusion lies over \(R\)]
764	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}
765	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}
766	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_right}
767	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right})
768	  intertwines the two structure maps:
769	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/y]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
770	\end{lemma}
771	\begin{proof}
772	
773	\leanok
774	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
775	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
776	\end{proof}
777	
778	\begin{lemma}
779	
780	\leanok
781	[Left factor is a unit in the double localisation]
782	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}
783	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}
784	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
785	  For \(x, y \in R\), the image of \(x\) in \(R[1/(xy)]\) is a unit.
786	\end{lemma}
787	\begin{proof}
788	
789	\leanok
790	  \(xy\) is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\)
791	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}); a factor of a unit is a unit.
792	  Proved directly in Lean.
793	\end{proof}
794	
795	\begin{lemma}
796	
797	\leanok
798	[Right factor is a unit in the double localisation]
799	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}
800	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}
801	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
802	  For \(x, y \in R\), the image of \(y\) in \(R[1/(xy)]\) is a unit.
803	\end{lemma}
804	\begin{proof}
805	
806	\leanok
807	  \(xy\) is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\)
808	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}); a factor of a unit is a unit.
809	  Proved directly in Lean.
810	\end{proof}
811	
812	\begin{lemma}
813	
814	\leanok
815	[The cross minor becomes a unit after inclusion]
816	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
817	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}

819	  Let \(A, B, C\) be size-\(d\) subsets and \(\iota : R^A_B \to D\) a ring
820	  homomorphism over the structure map \(R^A \to D\) under which the minor
821	  determinant \(P^A_C\) becomes a unit of \(D\). Then
822	  \(\iota\bigl(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C)\bigr)\) is a unit of \(D\). Indeed
823	  \[
824	    \tilde\theta_{A,B}\bigl(P^B_C\bigr)
825	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1} X^A_C\bigr)
826	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1}\bigr)\cdot P^A_C ,
827	  \]
828	  a product of two units once \(P^A_C\) is inverted in \(D\).
829	\end{lemma}

831	
832	\leanok
833	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet},
834	  \(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C) = \det(M_C)\) with \(M = (X^A_B)^{-1} X^A\); split
835	  the \(C\)-minor of the product (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) and the
836	  determinant as \(\det((X^A_B)^{-1}) \cdot P^A_C\). The first factor is a unit
837	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}); the second becomes a unit under
838	  \(\iota\) by hypothesis. Proved directly in Lean.
839	\end{proof}

1042	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
1043	\label{sec:gr_glued}
1044	
1045	\begin{definition}
1046	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1047	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
1048	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
1049	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1050	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
1051	  % affine patches" (read from
1052	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
1053	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1054	  % "Therefore the schemes
1055	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
1056	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
1057	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
1058	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
1059	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
1060	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
1061	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
1062	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1063	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
1064	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
1065	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
1066	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
1067	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
1068	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
1069	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
1070	  \[
1071	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
1072	  \]
1073	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
1074	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
1075	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
1076	
1077	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1078	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1079	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1080	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1081	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1082	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1083	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1084	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1085	  follows.
1086	\end{definition}

1088	\section{Separatedness}
1089	\label{sec:gr_separated}
1090	
1091	\begin{lemma}
1092	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1093	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
1094	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
1095	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1096	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
1097	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
1098	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1099	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
1100	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
1101	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
1102	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1103	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1104	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1105	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
1106	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
1107	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
1108	\end{lemma}
1109	
1110	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
1111	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
1112	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1113	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1114	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
1115	\begin{proof}
1116	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1117	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
1118	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
1119	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
1120	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
1121	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
1122	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
1123	  homomorphism is surjective.
1124	
1125	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
1126	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
1127	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
1128	  \[
1129	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
1130	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
1131	    \qquad
1132	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1133	  \]
1134	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
1135	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
1136	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
1137	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
1138	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
1139	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
1140	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
1141	  and in particular
1142	  \[
1143	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
1144	  \]
1145	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
1146	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
1147	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
1148	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
1149	  \[
1150	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
1151	  \]
1152	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
1153	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
1154	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
1155	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
1156	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
1157	\end{proof}

1160	\label{sec:gr_proper}
1161	
1162	\begin{lemma}
1163	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1164	  \label{lem:gr_proper}
1165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}
1166	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}
1167	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
1168	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891).
1169	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1170	  % "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
1171	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.

1177	  %  the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
1178	  %  $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
1179	  %  $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
1180	  %  lies in $GL_d(\K)$.

1184	  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
1185	  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
1186	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
1187	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
1188	  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
1189	  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
1190	  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
1191	  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
1192	  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
1193	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked

1195	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
1196	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}

1198	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is proper.

1200	
1201	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is
1202	% proper. It is enough to verify the valuative criterion of properness for
1203	% discrete valuation rings. Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
1204	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by a ring
1205	% homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed one such $I$, next
1206	% choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum, where
1207	% $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes the discrete valuation. As
1208	% $P^I_I = 1$, note that $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$
1209	% and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$ lies in $GL_d(\K)$. Now consider the homomorphism
1210	% $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1211	% $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$ Then $g$ defines the same morphism
1212	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$, and moreover all $d\times d$ minors
1213	% $X^J_K$ satisfy $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it
1214	% follows from the above that in fact $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of
1215	% $X^J$. Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ factors uniquely via
1216	% $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of schemes
1217	% $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
1218	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked separatedness of
1219	% $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
1220	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891.)
1221	\begin{proof}
1222	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
1223	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
1224	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
1225	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
1226	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
1227	  \[
1228	    \begin{array}{ccc}
1229	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
1230	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
1231	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
1232	    \end{array}
1233	  \]
1234	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
1235	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
1236	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1237	
1238	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
1239	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
1240	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
1241	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
1242	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
1243	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
1244	  \(K\). Define
1245	  \[
1246	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
1247	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
1248	  \]
1249	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
1250	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
1251	  landing in \(U^J\).
1252	
1253	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
1254	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
1255	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
1256	  \[
1257	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
1258	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
1259	    \qquad
1260	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
1261	  \]
1262	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
1263	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
1264	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
1265	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
1266	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
1267	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
1268	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
1269	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
1270	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
1271	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
1272	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
1273	
1274	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
1275	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
1276	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1277	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
1278	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
1279	  proper.
1280	\end{proof}

1282	\section{Out of scope}
1283	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
1284	
1285	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
1286	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
1287	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
1288	are not treated here:
1289	\begin{itemize}
1290	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
1291	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
1292	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
1293	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1294	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
1295	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
1296	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
1297	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
1298	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
1299	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
1300	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
1301	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
1302	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
1303	    that base change.
1304	\end{itemize}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}
159	
160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\begin{proof}
177	
178	\leanok
179	  Constructed directly in Lean as the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
180	  \(\mathcal{A}\); this is the projection underlying \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
181	\end{proof}
182	
183	\begin{theorem}\leanok
184	[Universal property of the relative spectrum]
185	  \label{thm:relative_spec_univ}
186	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}
187	  % NOTE iter-283: removed the spurious `thm:relative_spec_base_change` from this
188	  % \uses. The universal property is the PRIMITIVE statement, proved by reducing
189	  % to the affine-base case and gluing (Lean body of `UniversalProperty` uses
190	  % only `isAffineHom_of_forall_exists_isAffineOpen`/`relativeGluingData`, never

259	  % ``.toBase``; iter-179 Lane B closes the Lean ``UniversalProperty`` proof

266	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
267	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
268	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
269	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
270	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
271	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
272	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case
273	  (\cref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
274	  restriction identification \(F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}\).
275	  Each \(F_i \hookrightarrow F\) is an open
276	  subfunctor (pull back \(U_i\) along \(f\)). The \(F_i\) jointly cover \(F\) since the \(U_i\)
277	  cover \(X\). The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
278	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)
279	  of \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.

288	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.affine_base_iff}
289	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
290	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as the
291	  % weaker ``IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜)'' rather than the canonical
292	  % iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))'' pinned by this prose. The Lean name
293	  % ``affine_base_iff'' is also misleading (the encoded type is not an iff).
294	  % Iter-174+ refines to the iff-with-Γ statement once Γ : QcohAlgebra → CommRingCat
295	  % is available.
296	  
297	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine)
298	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L491-L545).
299	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
300	  % "In Situation REF.
301	  %  Let \(F\) be the functor associated to \((S, \mathcal{A})\) above.
302	  %  If \(S\) is affine, then \(F\) is representable by the
303	  %  affine scheme \(\Spec(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{A}))\)."
304	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine).}
305	  Let \(X = \Spec R\) be an affine scheme and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent
306	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Set \(A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\), regarded as an
307	  \(R\)-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(X\)-schemes
308	  \[
309	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
310	  \]
311	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
312	  spectrum of the global sections.
313	\end{theorem}

317	% The ring map \(R \to A\) gives rise to a canonical map

319	% f_{univ} : \Spec(A)
320	% \longrightarrow
321	% S = \Spec(R).
322	% \(\)
323	% We have
324	% \(f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} =  \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}\)
325	% by Schemes, Lemma REF.
326	% Hence there is a canonical map
327	% \(\)
328	% \varphi_{univ} :
329	% f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
330	% \longrightarrow
331	% \widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
332	% \(\)
333	% coming from the \(A\)-module map \(A \otimes_R A \to A\),
334	% \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We claim that the pair
335	% \((f_{univ}, \varphi_{univ})\) represents \(F\) in this case."

338	  \leanok
339	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
340	  % The iter-176 ``change IsAffine (Spec R); inferInstance`` form is now stale
341	  % — iter-179 Lane B closes via ``UniversalProperty 𝒜`` + ``isAffine_of_isAffineHom``
342	  % against the iter-179 Mathlib-backed ``RelativeSpec``/``structureMorphism``
343	  % bodies. The Lean type remains the strictly weaker ``IsAffine`` rather than

345	  % commitment; still pending iter-180+).
346	  Write \(X = \Spec R\) and \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\). Quasi-coherence of
347	  \(\mathcal{A}\) gives \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
348	  (with the \(R\)-algebra structure on \(A\) inducing the algebra structure on
349	  \(\widetilde A\)). The ring map \(R \to A\) gives a morphism
350	  \(f_{\mathrm{univ}} : \Spec A \to \Spec R = X\) and a canonical
351	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A}\)-algebra map
352	  $\varphi_{\mathrm{univ}} : f_{\mathrm{univ}}^* \mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
353	  \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec A} = \widetilde A$ given by multiplication
354	  \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We verify \((\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})\)
355	  represents \(F\) on the affine base: given a test scheme \(T\) and a pair
356	  \((f, \varphi)\), the composite
357	  $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
358	  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$
359	  is a ring map \(A \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\) and hence corresponds to a morphism
