1034	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
1035	
1036	\begin{definition}\leanok
1037	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
1038	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
1039	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
1040	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum}
1041	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.hilb}
1042	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1043	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
1044	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
1045	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
1046	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
1047	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
1048	  \begin{itemize}
1049	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
1050	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
1051	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
1052	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
1053	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
1054	    \item a finiteness condition: the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a finite module over the
1055	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
1056	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),
1057	      \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N'\) of \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}).
1058	  \end{itemize}
1059	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
1060	  Hilbert function} is
1061	  \[
1062	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1063	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
1064	  \]
1065	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
1066	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
1067	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
1068	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
1069	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
1070	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
1071	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
1072	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
1073	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
1074	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
1075	\end{definition}
1076	
1077	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
1078	
1079	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
1080	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
1081	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
1082	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
1083	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
1084	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1085	
1086	\begin{definition}\leanok
1087	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1088	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1089	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1090	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1091	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1092	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1093	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1094	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1095	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1096	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1097	\end{definition}
1098	
1099	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1100	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1101	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1102	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1103	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1104	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1105	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1106	\end{lemma}
1107	
1108	\begin{proof}
1109	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1110	  \leanok
1111	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1112	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1113	\end{proof}
1114	
1115	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1116	[Degree-shift commutation]
1117	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1119	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1120	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1121	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1122	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1123	  \[
1124	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1125	  \]
1126	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1127	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1128	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1129	\end{lemma}
1130	
1131	\begin{proof}
1132	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1133	  \leanok
1134	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1135	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1136	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1137	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1138	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1139	\end{proof}
1140	
1141	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1142	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1143	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1145	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1146	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1147	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1148	\end{lemma}
1149	
1150	\begin{proof}
1151	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1152	  \leanok
1153	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1154	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1155	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1156	\end{proof}
1157	
1158	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1159	[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1160	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1161	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1162	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1163	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1164	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1165	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1166	\end{lemma}
1167	
1168	\begin{proof}
1169	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1170	  \leanok
1171	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1172	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1173	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1174	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1175	  homogeneous.
1176	\end{proof}
1177	
1178	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1179	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1180	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1181	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1182	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1183	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1184	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1185	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1186	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1187	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1188	\end{lemma}
1189	
1190	\begin{proof}
1191	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1192	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1193	    \leanok
1194	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1195	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1196	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1197	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1198	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1199	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1200	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1201	  homogeneous.
1202	\end{proof}
1203	
1204	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1205	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1206	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1207	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1208	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1209	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1210	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1211	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1212	  this is recorded here.
1213	\end{lemma}
1214	
1215	\begin{proof}
1216	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1217	  \leanok
1218	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1219	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1220	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1221	\end{proof}
1222	
1223	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1224	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1225	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1226	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1227	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1228	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1229	\end{lemma}
1230	
1231	\begin{proof}
1232	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1233	  \leanok
1234	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1235	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1236	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1237	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1238	\end{proof}
1239	
1240	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1241	[Ambient image identity]
1242	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1244	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1245	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1246	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1247	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1248	  \[
1249	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1250	  \]
1251	\end{lemma}
1252	
1253	\begin{proof}
1254	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1255	  \leanok
1256	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1257	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1258	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1259	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1260	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1261	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1262	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1263	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1264	\end{proof}
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1267	[Ambient distributive law]
1268	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1269	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1270	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1271	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1272	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1273	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1274	  \[
1275	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1276	  \]
1277	\end{lemma}
1278	
1279	\begin{proof}
1280	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1281	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1282	    \leanok
1283	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1284	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1285	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1286	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1287	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1288	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1289	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1290	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1291	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1292	\end{proof}
1293	
1294	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1295	
1296	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1297	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1298	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1299	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1300	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1301	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1302	
1303	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1304	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1305	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1306	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1307	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1308	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1309	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1310	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1311	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1312	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1313	\end{lemma}
1314	
1315	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1316	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1317	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1318	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1319	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1320	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1321	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1322	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1323	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1324	\end{lemma}
1325	
1326	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1327	[The kernel pair nests]
1328	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1329	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1330	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1331	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1332	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1333	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1334	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1335	\end{lemma}
1336	
1337	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1338	[The cokernel pair nests]
1339	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1340	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1341	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1342	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1343	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1344	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1345	\end{lemma}
1346	
1347	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1348	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1349	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1350	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1351	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1352	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1353	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1354	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1355	\end{lemma}
1356	
1357	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1358	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1359	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1360	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1361	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1362	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1363	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1364	\end{lemma}
1365	
1366	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1367	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1368	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1370	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1371	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1372	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1373	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1374	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1375	\end{lemma}
1376	
1377	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1378	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1379	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1381	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1382	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1383	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1384	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1385	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1386	\end{lemma}
1387	
1388	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1389	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1390	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1391	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1392	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1393	  % length-(r-1) datum.
1394	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1395	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1396	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1397	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1398	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1399	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1400	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1401	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1402	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1403	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1404	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1405	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1406	  \[
1407	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1408	    \qquad
1409	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1410	  \]
1411	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1412	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1413	  \begin{enumerate}
1414	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1415	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1416	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1417	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1418	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1419	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1420	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1421	  \end{enumerate}
1422	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1423	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1424	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1425	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1426	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1427	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1428	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1429	\end{lemma}
1430	
1431	\begin{proof}
1432	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1433	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1434	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1435	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1436	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1437	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1438	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1439	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1440	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1441	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1442	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1443	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components

2549	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
2550	  % is an isomorphism
2551	  % $$
2552	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
2553	  % $$
2554	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
2555	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
2556	  % \end{enumerate}"
2557	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
2558	  \(i = 0\) case.}
2559	  Consider the cartesian square
2560	  \[
2561	    \begin{array}{ccc}
2562	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
2563	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
2564	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
2565	    \end{array}
2566	  \]
2567	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
2568	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
2569	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
2570	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
2571	  \[
2572	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2573	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
2574	  \]
2575	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
2576	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
2577	  \[
2578	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2579	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
2580	  \]
2581	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
2582	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
2583	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
2584	
2585	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
2586	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
2587	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
2588	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
2589	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
2590	  an isomorphism.
2591	\end{theorem}

2651	% NOTE: For the $i = 0$ case the argument is Čech-free: $H^0(X,\mathcal{F})$ is the
2652	% sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover (Čech degree 0/1, NOT Čech
2653	% cohomology -- no Leray, no spectral sequence), each term base-changes by the affine
2654	% lemma lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, and flatness of $A \to B$ commutes
2655	% $-\otimes_A B$ with that finite equalizer via lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib
2656	% (Mathlib's LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv). The quasi-separated case is the
2657	% Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number of cover members named in the source quote.
2658	\begin{proof}
2659	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2660	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
2661	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
2662	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
2663	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
2664	  sequence.
2665	
2666	  \medskip
2667	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
2668	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
2669	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
2670	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
2671	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
2672	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
2673	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
2674	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
2675	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
2676	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
2677	  \[
2678	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
2679	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
2680	  \]
2681	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
2682	
2683	  \medskip
2684	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
2685	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
2686	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
2687	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
2688	  \[
2689	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2690	    \;=\;
2691	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
2692	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
2693	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
2694	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
2695	    \Bigr),
2696	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
2697	  \]
2698	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
2699	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
2700	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
2701	  higher cohomology.
2702	
2703	  \medskip
2704	  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
2705	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
2706	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
2707	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
2708	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
2709	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
2710	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
2711	  \[
2712	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2713	    \qquad
2714	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2715	  \]
2716	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
2717	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
2718	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
2719	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
2720	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}):
2721	  \[
2722	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
2723	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
2724	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2725	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
2726	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
2727	  \]
2728	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
2729	
2730	  \medskip
2731	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
2732	  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
2733	  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
2734	  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
2735	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
2736	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
2737	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
2738	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
2739	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
2740	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
2741	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
2742	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
2743	  \[
2744	    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2745	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
2746	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
2747	  \]
2748	  and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness of \(A \to B\) makes
2749	  \(- \otimes_A B\) preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
2750	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
2751	  \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t - 1\) members), and for
2752	  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
2753	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
2754	\end{proof}

1566	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1567	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1568	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1569	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1570	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1571	
1572	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1573	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1574	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1575	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1576	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1577	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1578	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1579	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1580	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1581	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1582	
1583	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1584	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1585	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1586	  \[
1587	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1588	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1589	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1590	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1591	  \]
1592	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1593	\end{proof}

1595	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1596	
1597	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1598	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1599	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1600	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1601	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1602	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1603	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1604	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1605	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1606	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1607	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1608	
1609	\begin{definition}\leanok
1610	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1611	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1612	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1613	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1614	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1615	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1616	  ring homomorphism}
1617	  \[
1618	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1619	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1620	  \]
1621	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1622	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1623	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1624	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1625	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1626	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1627	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1628	\end{definition}
1629	
1630	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1631	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1632	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1633	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1634	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1635	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1636	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1637	  \(t_i\).
1638	\end{lemma}
1639	
1640	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1641	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]
1642	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1643	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_C}
1644	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1645	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\) for each \(c \in \kappa\):
1646	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and
1647	  carries the constant polynomial \(C\,c\) to the scalar endomorphism \(c \cdot 1\).
1648	\end{lemma}
1649	
1650	\begin{definition}\leanok
1651	[Polynomial-ring module structure on $M$]
1652	  \label{def:graded_polyModule}
1653	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule}
1654	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1655	  The \emph{polynomial-module structure} is the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module
1656	  structure on \(M\) obtained by restricting scalars along
1657	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}): a polynomial \(p\) acts on
1658	  \(m \in M\) by \(p \cdot m = \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m)\). This is the module over the
1659	  free polynomial ring on which the ambient subquotient induction's finiteness condition
1660	  is measured.
1661	\end{definition}
1662	
1663	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1664	[$X_i$ acts as $t_i$]
1665	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}
1666	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_X_smul}
1667	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1668	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \(X_i \cdot m =
1669	  t_i(m)\) for all \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1670	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}).
1671	\end{lemma}
1672	
1673	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1674	[A constant acts as a scalar]
1675	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1676	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_C_smul}
1677	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1678	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \((C\,c) \cdot m =
1679	  c \cdot m\) for all \(c \in \kappa\), \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1680	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\)
1681	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1682	\end{lemma}
1683	
1684	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1685	[$\kappa$-scalar tower]
1686	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1687	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_isScalarTower}
1688	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1689	  The polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}) is compatible with the
1690	  original \(\kappa\)-action: \(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\,\kappa\,\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1691	  t_{r-1}]\,M\). Indeed the algebra map \(\kappa \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1692	  followed by the polynomial action recovers the \(\kappa\)-action, since a scalar
1693	  \(c \in \kappa\) maps to the constant \(C\,c\), which acts as \(c\)
1694	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1695	\end{lemma}
1696	
1697	\begin{definition}\leanok
1698	[Polynomial-ring submodule from a $t$-stable submodule]
1699	  \label{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule}
1701	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul,
1702	    lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1703	  Let \(N \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule that is stable under each \(t_i\)
1704	  (\(t_i \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then \(N\), with the \emph{same} ambient carrier, is a
1705	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule of \(M\) for the polynomial-module structure
1706	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}). Stability under the action of an arbitrary polynomial
1707	  follows by induction on monomials: constants act as scalars
1708	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}) and multiplication by \(X_i\) acts as \(t_i\)
1709	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}), both of which preserve \(N\).
1710	\end{definition}
1711	
1712	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1713	[The polynomial submodule has the same carrier]
1714	  \label{lem:graded_polySubmodule_coe}
1715	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule_coe}
1716	  \uses{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1717	  The underlying set of \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}(t, N)\)
1718	  (\cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}) equals \(N\): the lift to a
1719	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule does not change the carrier, so every object
1720	  in the induction remains an ambient submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1721	\end{lemma}

1723	\subsubsection*{Finiteness-transfer infrastructure}
1724	
1725	The ambient finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer})
1726	factors the polynomial action of the larger ring through a surjection onto the
1727	smaller one. The following blocks build that surjection and the semilinearity
1728	identity it rests on, and record the two elementary finiteness manipulations
1729	(shrinking a numerator, enlarging a denominator) used to feed the kernel and
1730	cokernel constructors.
1731	
1732	\begin{definition}
1733	
1734	\leanok
1735	[The last-variable surjection of polynomial rings]
1736	  \label{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1737	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom}
1738	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_castSucc}
1739	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_last}
1740	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_C}
1741	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_rename_castSucc}
1742	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_surjective}
1743	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_ringHomSurjective}
1744	  \uses{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1745	  For a field \(\kappa\) and \(r \in \mathbb{N}\), the \emph{last-variable
1746	  homomorphism} is the \(\kappa\)-algebra map
1747	  \[
1748	    \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1749	      = \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r\}, \kappa)
1750	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1751	      \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r-1\}, \kappa) = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1752	  \]
1753	  obtained by evaluation: it sends the last variable \(X_r\) to \(0\) and each
1754	  earlier variable \(X_i\) (for \(0 \le i < r\)) to \(X_i\), and fixes constants.
1755	  It is a left inverse of the algebra map
1756	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1757	  induced by the canonical inclusion \(\{0, \dots, r-1\} \hookrightarrow \{0, \dots, r\}\)
1758	  on variable indices, and is therefore surjective. This is the concrete
1759	  ring surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1760	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) along which finiteness is transferred
1761	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
1762	\end{definition}
1763	
1764	\begin{lemma}
1765	
1766	\leanok
1767	[A $t$-stable submodule is closed under the polynomial action]
1768	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}
1769	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}
1770	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1771	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms
1772	  of \(M\) and let \(P' \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule stable under each
1773	  \(t_i\) (\(t_i \cdot P' \subseteq P'\)). Then \(P'\) is closed under the action of
1774	  every polynomial: for all \(p \in \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and \(m \in P'\),
1775	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m) \in P'\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}). The proof
1776	  is induction on \(p\): a constant acts as a scalar (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}),
1777	  multiplication by a variable acts as the stabilising \(t_i\)
1778	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}), and sums are handled additively.
1779	\end{lemma}
1780	
1781	\begin{lemma}
1782	
1783	\leanok
1784	[Mod-$P'$ semilinearity of the polynomial action]
1785	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}
1786	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}
1787	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C,
1788	    lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable, lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1789	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1790	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules each stable under every
1791	  \(t_i\), with the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carrying \(P\) into \(P'\)
1792	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)). Then for every polynomial
1793	  \(s \in \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) and every \(m \in P\),
1794	  \[
1795	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(s)(m)
1796	      \;-\; \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})
1797	        \bigl(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}(s)\bigr)(m)
1798	      \;\in\; P' .
1799	  \]
1800	  In words: acting by \(s\) through the full family \(t\) agrees, modulo \(P'\), with
1801	  first projecting away the last variable via \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\)
1802	  (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}) and acting through the reduced family
1803	  \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). This is the semilinearity identity that makes the
1804	  \(\sigma\)-semilinear quotient map of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1805	  well-defined. The proof is induction on \(s\): the case of an earlier variable
1806	  \(s = X_i\) (\(i < r\)) is the inductive hypothesis after \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\)
1807	  fixes it; the case of the last variable \(s = X_r\) annihilates the reduced term,
1808	  and the full term lands in \(P'\) because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into the
1809	  \(t\)-stable \(P'\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}).
1810	\end{lemma}
1811	
1812	\begin{lemma}
1813	
1814	\leanok
1815	[Shrinking the numerator preserves finiteness]
1816	  \label{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1817	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1818	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1819	    lem:module_finite_of_injective_mathlib,
1820	    lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib,
1821	    lem:isNoetherian_of_isNoetherianRing_of_finite_mathlib}
1822	  Let \(N_1 \le N_2\) and \(P'\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1823	  the commuting family \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and suppose the subquotient
1824	  \(N_2/P'\) is finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1825	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}, \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(N_1/P'\) is
1826	  also finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\). Indeed the inclusion
1827	  \(N_1 \subseteq N_2\) induces an injective \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear
1828	  map \(N_1/P' \hookrightarrow N_2/P'\); since
1829	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) is Noetherian
1830	  (\cref{lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib}), the finite module
1831	  \(N_2/P'\) is Noetherian
1832	  (\cref{lem:isNoetherian_of_isNoetherianRing_of_finite_mathlib}), so its submodule
1833	  \(N_1/P'\) is finite (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_injective_mathlib}). This supplies
1834	  the finiteness witness of the kernel constructor
1835	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}).
1836	\end{lemma}
1837	
1838	\begin{lemma}
1839	
1840	\leanok
1841	[Enlarging the denominator preserves finiteness]
1842	  \label{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
1843	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
1844	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1845	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1846	  Let \(N\) and \(P_1 \le P_2\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1847	  the commuting family \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and suppose the subquotient
1848	  \(N/P_1\) is finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1849	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}, \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(N/P_2\) is
1850	  also finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\). Indeed the inclusion
1851	  \(P_1 \subseteq P_2\) gives a surjection \(N/P_1 \twoheadrightarrow N/P_2\)
1852	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}), and finiteness transfers along it
1853	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}). This supplies the
1854	  finiteness witness of the cokernel constructor
1855	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}).
1856	\end{lemma}
1857	
1858	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1859	
1860	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1861	[Abstract single-pair finiteness transfer down one variable]
1862	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1863	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1864	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1865	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1866	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib,
1867	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1868	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1869	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1870	  every \(t_i\). Suppose the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\)
1871	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)), and that the subquotient \(P/P'\) is finite over the
1872	  free polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) for the polynomial-module structure
1873	  of \cref{def:graded_polyModule} (with carriers the ambient \(t\)-stable submodules
1874	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\), \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\),
1875	  \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(P/P'\) is finite over
1876	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\).
1877	
1878	  This is the abstract single-pair transfer: it does not mention the kernel/cokernel
1879	  pairs, but is the engine applied to each of them by the kernel and cokernel
1880	  constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
1881	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}). Because the last variable
1882	  annihilates the subquotient, the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-action factors through
1883	  the last-variable surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1884	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), which is what makes the transfer drop one variable.
1885	\end{lemma}
1886	
1887	\begin{proof}
1888	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1889	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1890	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1891	    \leanok
1892	  Write \(\sigma = \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1893	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the surjective ring
1894	  homomorphism sending the last variable to \(0\) and each earlier variable \(X_i\)
1895	  (\(i < r\)) to \(X_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}). Consider the map on numerators
1896	  \[
1897	    g : \mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P \;\longrightarrow\;
1898	      \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/
1899	        \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr), \qquad y \longmapsto [\,y\,],
1900	  \]
1901	  where the source carries the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-module structure and the
1902	  target the reduced \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module structure (both with the
1903	  same ambient carrier \(P\), \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). The mod-\(P'\)
1904	  semilinearity identity (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}) -- acting by
1905	  \(s\) through the full family and acting by \(\sigma(s)\) through the reduced family
1906	  agree modulo \(P'\), precisely because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\) --
1907	  says exactly that \(g\) is \(\sigma\)-semilinear. The map \(g\) is surjective (it is
1908	  the quotient projection on the common carrier), and it kills the denominator
1909	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\), so it descends through that submodule
1910	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}) to a surjective
1911	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear map
1912	  \[
1913	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1914	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\;
1915	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1916	  \]
1917	  from the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finite subquotient onto the reduced one.
1918	  Finiteness transfers along this surjection
1919	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}), giving finiteness of \(P/P'\)
1920	  over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\).
1921	
1922	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1923	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), not the subalgebra
1924	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq
1925	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1926	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection
1927	  \(\sigma\) that the scalar transfer requires
1928	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}). Keeping free polynomial
1929	  rings at every step keeps \(\sigma\) -- and hence the transfer -- available
1930	  throughout. Everything is finite generation of ambient submodules of the fixed
1931	  \(M\) and their quotients.
1932	\end{proof}
1933	
1934	\subsubsection*{Subquotient constructors}
1935	
1936	The kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and the
1937	abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1938	bundled into two constructors that take a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum to the
1939	length-\(r\) kernel and cokernel data consumed by the induction. The two stability
1940	lemmas below first record that the new pairs are stable under the \emph{whole}
1941	endomorphism family (not merely the reduced one), which is what lets the over-\(\kappa[t_0,\dots,t_r]\)
1942	finiteness inputs be stated before the variable is dropped.
1943	
1944	\begin{lemma}
1945	
1946	\leanok
1947	[The kernel pair's lower module is stable under the full family]
1948	  \label{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}
1949	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_stable_full}
1950	  \uses{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1951	  Let \((N, N')\) be a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum
1952	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and
1953	  \(x = t_r\). Then the kernel pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is stable
1954	  under \emph{every} \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)), not only the reduced family: each
1955	  \(t_i\) preserves \(N\) and preserves the preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) because it
1956	  commutes with \(x\) and preserves \(N'\)
1957	  (\cref{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}). This full stability is what allows the
1958	  kernel subquotient's finiteness to be measured over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1959	  before the transfer drops the last variable.
1960	\end{lemma}
1961	
1962	\begin{lemma}
1963	
1964	\leanok
1965	[The cokernel pair's upper module is stable under the full family]
1966	  \label{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}
1967	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_stable_full}
1968	  \uses{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1969	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1970	  stable under every \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)): each \(t_i\) preserves \(N'\) and
1971	  preserves the image \(x \cdot N\) because it commutes with \(x\) and preserves
1972	  \(N\) (\cref{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}), and a map preserves a sum of
1973	  preserved submodules. This full stability supports the over-\(\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1974	  t_r]\) finiteness input of the cokernel constructor.
1975	\end{lemma}
1976	
1977	\begin{definition}
1978	
1979	\leanok
1980	[Kernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
1981	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}
1982	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1983	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1984	    lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1985	    lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_ker_stable_full,
1986	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
1987	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1988	  From a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum \((N, N')\) with endomorphisms
1989	  \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and \(x = t_r\) (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), the
1990	  \emph{kernel constructor} produces the length-\(r\) datum
1991	  \[
1992	    \ker D \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr)
1993	  \]
1994	  on the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). Its
1995	  non-finiteness fields are supplied by the ambient kernel calculus: homogeneity of
1996	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}), the nesting
1997	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_le}), and stability under the
1998	  reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}). The finiteness witness
1999	  is obtained by first shrinking the numerator from \(N\) to
2000	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}) to get
2001	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the kernel subquotient, then transferring
2002	  down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}), valid because
2003	  \(x\) annihilates the kernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}). This
2004	  realises the kernel half of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine
2005	  length-\(r\) datum.
2006	\end{definition}
2007	
2008	\begin{definition}
2009	
2010	\leanok
2011	[Cokernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
2012	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}
2013	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.coker}
2014	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2015	    lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_coker_annihilate,
2016	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_coker_stable_full,
2017	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
2018	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
2019	  From the same length-\((r+1)\) datum, the \emph{cokernel constructor} produces the
2020	  length-\(r\) datum
2021	  \[
2022	    \operatorname{coker} D \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr)
2023	  \]
2024	  on the reduced family. Its non-finiteness fields come from the ambient cokernel
2025	  calculus: homogeneity of \(N' + x \cdot N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}),
2026	  the nesting \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_le}), and stability
2027	  under the reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}). The finiteness field
2028	  is obtained by enlarging the denominator from \(N'\) to \(N' + x \cdot N\)
2029	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}) to get
2030	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the cokernel subquotient, then
2031	  transferring down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}),
2032	  valid because \(x\) annihilates the cokernel subquotient
2033	  (\cref{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}). This realises the cokernel half of
2034	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine length-\(r\) datum.
2035	\end{definition}
2036	
2037	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
2038	
2039	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2040	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
2041	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
2042	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
2043	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2044	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2045	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
2046	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2047	  \[
2048	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2049	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2050	  \]
2051	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2052	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2053	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2054	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2055	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2056	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2057	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2058	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2059	  \[
2060	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2061	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2062	  \]
2063	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2064	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2065	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2066	\end{lemma}
2067	
2068	\begin{proof}
2069	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2070	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2071	    \leanok
2072	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2073	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2074	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2075	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2076	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2077	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2078	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2079	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2080	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2081	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2082	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2083	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and

2307	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2308	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2309	
2310	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2311	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2312	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2313	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2314	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2315

2317	
2318	\leanok

2325	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2326	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2327	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2328	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2329	  % Polynomials" (read from
2330	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).

2334	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2335	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2336	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2337	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2338	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2339	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2340	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2341	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2342	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2343	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2344	  \[
2345	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2346	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2347	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2348	  \]
2349	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2350	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2351	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).

2353	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2354	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2355	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2356	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2357	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2358	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2359	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2360	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2361	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2362	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2363	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2364	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2365	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2366	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2367	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2368	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2369	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2370	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2371	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2372	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2373	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2374	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2375	
2376	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2377	\end{definition}
2378	
2379	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2380	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
2381	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
2382	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
2383	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
2384	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
2385	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
2386	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
2387	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
2388	  \(U\):
2389	  \[
2390	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2391	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
2392	  \]
2393	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
2394	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
2395	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
2396	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
2397	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
2398	\end{lemma}

2400	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2401	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2402	  localization]
2403	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2404	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2405	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2406	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2407	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2408	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2409	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2410	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2411	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2412	  \[
2413	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2414	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2415	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2416	  \]
2417	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2418	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2419	
2420	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2421	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2422	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2423	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2424	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2425	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2426	\end{lemma}
2427	
2428	\begin{proof}
2429	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2430	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2431	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2432	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2433	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2434	
2435	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2436	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2437	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2438	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2439	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2440	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2441	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2442	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2443	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2444	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2445	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2446	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2447	\end{proof}
2448	
2449	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
2450	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2451	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
2452	  \mathlibok
2453	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2454	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
2455	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
2456	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
2457	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
2458	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
2459	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
2460	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
2461	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
2462	\end{lemma}
2463	
2464	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
2465	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2466	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
2467	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2468	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
2469	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
2470	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
2471	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2472	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
2473	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
2474	  %  associated to $M$.
2475	  %  [...]
2476	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
2477	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
2478	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2479	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2480	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2481	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2482	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2483	  \begin{enumerate}
2484	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2485	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2486	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2487	    \item the section-restriction map
2488	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2489	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2490	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2491	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2492	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2493	  \end{enumerate}
2494	\end{lemma}
2495	
2496	\begin{proof}
2497	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2498	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2499	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2500	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2501	
2502	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2503	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2504	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2505	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2506	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2507	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2508	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2509	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2510	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2511	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2512	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2513	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2514	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2515	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2516	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2517	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2518	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2519	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
2520	\end{proof}

2523	
2524	\leanok

2526	  \label{def:schematic_support}

2529	    def:schematic_support_immersion}
2530	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2531	  % Polynomials" (read from
2532	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2533	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2534	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,

2538	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}

2540	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)
2541	  cut out by its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):

2544	    \;=\; V\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
2545	  \]
2546	  equipped with its canonical closed immersion into \(X\). This realises

2548	  \(X\).

2551	\begin{definition}\leanok
2552	[Schematic-support immersion]
2553	  \label{def:schematic_support_immersion}
2554	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupportι}
2555	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:modules_annihilator}
2556	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
2557	  \emph{schematic-support immersion} is the canonical closed immersion
2558	  \[

2560	  \]
2561	  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) into the ambient
2562	  scheme, namely the subscheme immersion of the annihilator ideal sheaf

2564	  quasi-compact preimmersion onto the support, and it is the morphism whose
2565	  composite with a base morphism \(f : X \to S\) defines proper support
2566	  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}). Proved directly in Lean.

2569	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
2570	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
2571	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
2572	  \mathlibok
2573	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2574	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
2575	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
2576	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
2577	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
2578	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
2579	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
2580	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
2581	\end{lemma}

2583	\begin{definition}
2584	
2585	\leanok
2586	[Proper support over the base]
2587	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
2588	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
2589	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:schematic_support_immersion, lem:isProper_mathlib}
2590	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
2591	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
2592	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
2593	  % + L418-L419).
2594	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2595	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
2596	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
2597	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
2598	  %  [...]
2599	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
2600	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
2601	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2602	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
2603	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
2604	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
2605	  \[
2606	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
2607	  \]
2608	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
2609	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back

2611	  functor's pullback action requires.
