318	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
319	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
320	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
321	  \mathlibok
322	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
323	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
324	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
325	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
326	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
327	\end{lemma}
328	
329	\begin{lemma}
330	
331	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
332	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
334	  % NOTE (shared engine): the denominator-clearing performed here -- pushing finitely
335	  % many coefficients of one polynomial from K = Frac(A) down into a single
336	  % Localization.Away g -- is the same primitive the torsion reindex (L5b,
337	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex) uses when it inverts the leading coefficient of the
338	  % Nagata-normalised annihilator. A prover who writes one general "clear finitely many
339	  % denominators into one Localization.Away g" helper can have both lemmas consume it.
340	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
341	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
342	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
343	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
344	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
345	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
346	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
347	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
348	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
349	  %   Let K := FractionRing A and A_g := Localization.Away g; the canonical map
350	  %   A_g -> K (induced because powers of g are non-zero-divisors of A) extends along
351	  %   MvPolynomial.map to MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g -> MvPolynomial (Fin n) K.
352	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
353	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
354	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0),
355	  %         p ∈ Set.range
356	  %           (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)))
357	  % NOTE (encoding): there is NO canonical `Algebra (Localization.Away g)
358	  %   (FractionRing A)` instance, so the landed Lean does not use the literal
359	  %   `algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)` above. It encodes the same map
360	  %   as `IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A) hle` with
361	  %   `hle : powers g ≤ comap id (nonZeroDivisors A)` — mathematically identical, just the
362	  %   well-typed form. The proof is axiom-clean.
363	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
364	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
365	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
366	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
367	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
368	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
369	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
370	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
371	\end{lemma}
372	
373	\begin{proof}
374	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
375	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
376	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
377	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
378	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
379	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
380	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
381	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
382	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
383	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
384	\end{proof}
385	
386	\begin{lemma}[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
387	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
388	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
389	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator,
390	        lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
391	  % NOTE: the LEAN SIGNATURE block below now matches the landed
392	  % Lean decl `exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial`, which RETAINS the binder
393	  % `(_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))` as
394	  % the 3rd existential (after `(_ : g ≠ 0)`, before `φ`) — required to type the
395	  % AlgHom target as an A_g-algebra. The MATH statement is unaffected.
396	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (landed target, AlgHom encoding). Write
397	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g,   B_g := Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g).
398	  % The conclusion is:
399	  %   ∃ (n : ℕ) (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
400	  %     (_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
401	  %     (φ : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g)
402	  %           →ₐ[Localization.Away g] Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)),
403	  %     Function.Injective φ ∧
404	  %     (letI := φ.toAlgebra;
405	  %      Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g))
406	  %                    (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g))) ∧
407	  %     (∀ a, algebraMap A_g B_g (algebraMap A A_g a)
408	  %             = algebraMap B B_g (algebraMap A B a))
409	  % NOTE (iter-022): a FOURTH existential conjunct was added — the chosen
410	  % `algBg : Algebra A_g B_g` is COMPATIBLE with the canonical `A → B → B_g`
411	  % tower. Without it the existential's `Algebra A_g B_g` is arbitrary and the
412	  % downstream `genericFlatnessAlgebraic` step-4 bridge cannot prove
413	  % `IsScalarTower A A_g C_g`. The MATH statement is unaffected (the compatible
414	  % structure is the `awayMap` one); the conjunct just pins it across the ∃.
415	  % The polynomial subalgebra A_g[b_1,…,b_n] is realised as the DOMAIN
416	  % `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`, embedded into B_g by the algebra map φ; the
417	  % generators b_j are φ(X_j). Compared with an earlier draft using three anonymous
418	  % instance existentials (∃ (_ : Algebra A_g B_g) (_ : Algebra (MvPoly … A_g) B_g)
419	  % (_ : IsScalarTower …)), this form keeps only the single `Algebra A_g B_g` binder
420	  % needed to type B_g as an A_g-algebra: the single φ then carries the algebra map,
421	  % `Function.Injective φ` encodes algebraic independence of the b_j, and
422	  % `Module.Finite … over φ.toAlgebra` encodes module-finiteness of B_g over the
423	  % image polynomial subalgebra. Downstream, L5 (\cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core})
424	  % consumes a finite module over `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`; the AlgHom hands it
425	  % exactly that algebra structure via `φ.toAlgebra`, with no instance revival.
426	  % Why B_g and not B: generic flatness only needs the property after one
427	  % inversion g; the integral-dependence equations only clear denominators after
428	  % inverting g (see the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF below).
429	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
430	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
431	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
432	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
433	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
434	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
435	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
436	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
437	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
438	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
439	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
440	  normalisation with denominators).}
441	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
442	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
443	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
444	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
445	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
446	  \[
447	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
448	  \]
449	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
450	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
451	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
452	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
453	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
454	\end{lemma}
455	
456	\begin{proof}
457	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
458	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
459	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
460	  % NOTE: the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a Finset-fold over the finite
461	  % generating set sigma, but each fold step is the Mathlib collapsing lemma
462	  % IsIntegral.exists_multiple_integral_of_isLocalization applied to the generator AS A
463	  % WHOLE (not to its individual coefficient polynomials via gf_clear_one_denominator).
464	  % For each generator x, that lemma yields a single a_x in A^0 (nonzero, since A is a
465	  % domain) with C(a_x) * (1 (x) x) integral over MvPolynomial A; set g1 := prod_x a_x and
466	  % g := g0 * g1. The AlgHom assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
467	
468	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
469	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
470	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
471	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
472	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
473	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
474	
475	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
476	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
477	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
478	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
479	  \(x \in \sigma\) apply the Mathlib collapsing lemma
480	  \texttt{IsIntegral.exists\_multiple\_integral\_of\_isLocalization} (with
481	  \(R = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,A\), \(R_m = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,K\), \(S = B_K\), and the localizing
482	  submonoid the image of \(A^{0}\)): this returns a single
483	  \(a_x \in A^{0}\) (non-zero, as \(A\) is a domain) such that \(C(a_x)\cdot(1\otimes x)\)
484	  is integral over \(\mathrm{MvPoly}\,A = A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
485	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
486	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
487	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
488	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
489	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
490	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the landed proof discharges a whole generator
491	  at once via the collapsing lemma, which folds that primitive over the coefficients
492	  internally.)
493	
494	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
495	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
496	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
497	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
498	
499	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
500	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
501	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
502	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
503	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
504	  \[
505	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
506	  \]
507	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
508	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
509	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
510	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
511	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
512	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
513	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
514	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
515	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
516	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
517	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
518	
519	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
520	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
521	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
522	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
523	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
524	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
525	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
526	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
527	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
528	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
529	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
530	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
531	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
532	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
533	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
534	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
535	
536	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
537	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
538	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
539	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
540	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
541	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
542	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
543	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
544	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
545	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
546	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
547	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
548	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
549	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
550	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
551	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
552	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
553	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
554	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
555	\end{proof}
556	
557	\begin{lemma}[injectivity of a localisation lift]
558	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
559	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
560	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
561	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
562	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
563	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
564	\end{lemma}
565	\begin{proof}
566	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
567	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
568	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
569	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
570	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
571	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
572	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
573	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
574	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
575	\end{proof}
576	
577	\begin{lemma}
578	
579	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
580	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
581	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
582	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
583	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
584	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
585	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
586	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
587	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
588	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
589	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
590	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
591	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
592	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
593	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
594	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
595	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
596	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
597	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
598	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
599	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
600	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
601	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
602	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
603	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
604	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
605	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
606	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
607	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
608	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
609	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
610	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
611	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
612	  \[
613	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
614	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
615	  \]
616	
617	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
618	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
619	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
620	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
621	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
622	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
623	\end{lemma}
624	
625	\begin{proof}
626	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
627	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
628	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
629	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
630	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
631	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
632	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
633	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
634	
635	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
636	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
637	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
638	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
639	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
640	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
641	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
642	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
643	
644	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
645	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
646	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
647	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
648	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
649	\end{proof}

661	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
662	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
663	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
664	  \mathlibok
665	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
666	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
667	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
668	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
669	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
670	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
671	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
672	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
673	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
674	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
675	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
676	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
677	\end{lemma}

679	\begin{lemma}[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
680	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
681	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
682	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
683	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
684	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
685	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
686	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
687	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
688	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
689	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
690	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
691	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
692	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
693	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
694	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
695	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
696	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
697	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
698	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
699	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
700	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
701	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
702	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
703	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
704	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
705	\end{lemma}
706	
707	\begin{proof}
708	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
709	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
710	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
711	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
712	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
713	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
714	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
715	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
716	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
717	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
718	\end{proof}

720	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
721	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
722	
723	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
724	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
725	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
726	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
727	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
728	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
729	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
730	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
731	
732	\begin{definition}[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
733	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
734	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
735	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
736	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
737	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
738	  \[
739	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
740	  \]
741	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
742	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
743	\end{definition}
744	
745	\begin{lemma}[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
746	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
748	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
749	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
750	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
751	\end{lemma}
752	\begin{proof}
753	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
754	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
755	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\). Proved directly
756	  in Lean.
757	\end{proof}
758	
759	\begin{definition}[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
760	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
761	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
762	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
763	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
764	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
765	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
766	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
767	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
768	\end{definition}
769	
770	\begin{lemma}[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
771	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
772	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
773	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
774	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
775	\end{lemma}
776	\begin{proof}
777	  Proved directly in Lean.
778	\end{proof}
779	
780	\begin{lemma}[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
781	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
782	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
783	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
784	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
785	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
786	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
787	\end{lemma}
788	\begin{proof}
789	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
790	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
791	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
792	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
793	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
794	\end{proof}
795	
796	\begin{lemma}[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
797	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
798	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
799	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
800	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
801	  transformed monomial is the radix value
802	  \[
803	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
804	  \]
805	\end{lemma}
806	\begin{proof}
807	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
808	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
809	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
810	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
811	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
812	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
813	\end{proof}
814	
815	\begin{lemma}[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
816	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
817	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
818	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
819	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
820	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
821	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
822	\end{lemma}
823	\begin{proof}
824	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
825	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
826	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
827	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
828	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
829	\end{proof}
830	
831	\begin{lemma}[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
832	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
833	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
834	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
835	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
836	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
837	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
838	\end{lemma}
839	\begin{proof}
840	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
841	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
842	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
843	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
844	\end{proof}
845	
846	\begin{lemma}[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
847	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
848	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
849	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
850	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
851	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
852	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
853	\end{lemma}
854	\begin{proof}
855	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
856	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
857	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
858	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
859	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
860	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
861	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
862	\end{proof}
863	
864	\begin{lemma}[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
865	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
866	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GradedHilbertSerre.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\begin{definition}
200	[Section graded module]
201	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
203	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
205	  % Polynomials" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
209	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
210	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
211	  %  the function
212	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
213	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
214	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
215	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
216	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
217	  I.7.}
218	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
219	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
220	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
221	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
222	  \[
223	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
224	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
225	  \]
226	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
227	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
228	  by the section tensor product
229	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
230	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
231	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
232	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
233	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
234	\end{definition}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}
282	
283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
326	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
327	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
328	  \mathlibok
329	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
330	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
331	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
332	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
333	  inverse
334	  \[
335	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
336	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
337	  \]
338	  Together with the coercion
339	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
340	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
341	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
342	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
344	\end{lemma}

346	\begin{lemma}\leanok
347	[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
348	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
350	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,

356	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
358	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
