1242	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1243	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1244	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1245	% used in the codomain-read computation.
1246	
1247	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1248	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1249	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1250	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1251	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1252	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1253	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1254	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1255	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1256	\end{lemma}
1257	\begin{proof}
1258	
1259	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1260	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1261	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1262	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1263	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1264	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1265	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1266	\end{proof}
1267	
1268	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1269	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1270	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1271	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1272	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1273	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1274	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1275	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1276	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1277	\end{lemma}
1278	\begin{proof}
1279	
1280	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1281	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1282	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1283	\end{proof}

1285	% --- Section-level identity, three pieces: regroup equiv (pure tensor algebra, native
1286	%     R'-linear via Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange) ← section identity (Γ(θ) is the
1287	%     R'-base change of the algebraic unit) ← IsIso corollary (the pinned leaf). ---

1289	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1290	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1291	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1292	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1293	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1294	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1295	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1296	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1297	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1298	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1299	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1300	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1301	  % L933--938).
1302	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1303	  % equality
1304	  % $$
1305	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1306	  % $$
1307	  % as $R'$-modules."
1308	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1309	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1310	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1311	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1312	  \[
1313	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1314	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1315	  \]
1316	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1317	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1318	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1319	  hypothesis.
1320	\end{lemma}
1321	
1322	\begin{proof}
1323	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1324	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1325	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the standalone geometry-free helper
1326	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}, whose construction is now the
1327	  natively \(R'\)-linear pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1328	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1329	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1330	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1331	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1332	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1333	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1334	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1335	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1336	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1337	
1338	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1339	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1340	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1341	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1342	  dictionaries,
1343	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1344	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1345	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1346	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1347	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1348	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1349	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1350	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1351	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1352	  \[
1353	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1354	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1355	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1356	  \]
1357	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1358	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1359	
1360	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1361	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1362	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1363	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1364	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1365	\end{proof}

1510	\begin{lemma}
1511	
1512	\leanok
1513	[Pseudofunctoriality of the pullback--pushforward unit]
1514	  \label{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1516	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1517	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1518	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to X_3\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
1519	  \(N\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_3}\)-module. Write \(\eta^{h}\) for the unit of the
1520	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \((h^{*}\dashv h_{*})\) attached to a morphism \(h\), and
1521	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\), \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b}\) for the pseudofunctor
1522	  composition coherences. Then
1523	  \[
1524	    \eta^{b}_{N} \;\circ\; b_{*}\bigl(\eta^{a}_{\,b^{*}N}\bigr)
1525	      \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{N}
1526	    \;=\;
1527	    \eta^{a\circ b}_{N} \;\circ\;
1528	      (a\circ b)_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_{N}\bigr),
1529	  \]
1530	  where composites are read left to right. In words: the unit of the composite morphism
1531	  \(a \circ b\), reindexed by the inverse pullback coherence, factors as the \(b\)-unit, followed
1532	  by \(b_{*}\) of the \(a\)-unit, followed by the pushforward coherence. This is the leg-reindex
1533	  engine consumed by Seam~2 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}): with \(a\) an
1534	  isomorphism and \(b\) an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map it turns the generic projection-leg
1535	  unit into the affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-unit modulo the transparent coherences.

1537	\begin{proof}
1538	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1539	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1540	  Apply the unit-across-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib} to the
1541	  composite adjunction \((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}) . (a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\), the adjunction
1542	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1543	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1544	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by
1545	  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} that mate is exactly the pushforward
1546	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}\). Expanding the unit of the composite
1547	  adjunction by Lemma~\ref{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib} —

1549	    = \eta^{b}_N \circ b_{*}(\eta^{a}_{b^{*}N})\) — and reassociating the resulting composite gives
1550	  the stated equation.
1551	\end{proof}

1553	% --- Seam 2, step (ii): Gamma-collapse of the transparent pushforward coherences ---
1554	% These three project-bespoke atoms record that, on global sections over Spec R, the two
1555	% pushforward-composition coherences and the pushforward-congruence coherence appearing in
1556	% the inner composite are transparent (their section value is the identity / a presheaf
1557	% transport), hence collapse under the global-sections functor.

1559	\begin{lemma}
1560	
1561	\leanok
1562	[$\Gamma$-collapse of the pushforward-composition coherence (hom factor)]
1563	  \label{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}
1564	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}
1565	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
1566	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to \operatorname{Spec} R\) be composable morphisms of
1567	  schemes and \(M\) an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_1}\)-module. Applying the global-sections functor
1568	  \(\Gamma\) (over the base \(R\)) to the component
1569	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_M\) of the pushforward-composition coherence
1570	  isomorphism yields the identity morphism:
1571	  \[
1572	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_M\bigr) = \operatorname{id}.
1573	  \]
1574	  The pushforward-composition coherence is transparent at the level of sections.
1575	\end{lemma}
1576	\begin{proof}
1577	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
1578	  The component \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_M\) evaluates to the identity
1579	  on every open, and the global-sections functor sends the identity to the identity.
1580	\end{proof}

1638	  \emph{Superseded.} This lemma is a link of an abandoned first route to the section
1639	  identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_section_identity}), replaced by the direct
1640	  domain-read route — Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read} (domain read),
1641	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} (codomain read), and
1642	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose} (the \(g^*\)-transpose crux); it is
1643	  retained only pending dead-code removal.
1644	
1645	  Let \(e : X' \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Spec}(A \otimes_R R')\) be the canonical
1646	  isomorphism, \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\) the left tensor inclusion, and set
1647	  \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\). For an \(A\)-module \(M\), the bare \((g')\)-unit
1648	  \(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\) of the \((g')\)-adjunction expands as the four-factor composite
1649	  \[
1650	    \eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}
1651	      \;=\;
1652	      \eta^{\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}_{\widetilde M}
1653	      \,\circ\, (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}\bigl(\eta^{e}_{(\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^{*}\widetilde M}\bigr)
1654	      \,\circ\, (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{\widetilde M}
1655	      \,\circ\, (g')_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{\widetilde M}\bigr),
1656	  \]
1657	  where composites are read left to right.
1658	\end{lemma}
1659	\begin{proof}
1660	  \uses{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1661	  The leg-reindex engine Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}, instantiated at \(a = e\),
1662	  \(b = \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), \(N = \widetilde M\), reads
1663	  \[
1664	    \eta^{\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}_{\widetilde M}
1665	      \circ (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}(\eta^{e})
1666	      \circ (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}
1667	    \;=\;
1668	    \eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}
1669	      \circ (g')_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{inv}}\bigr).
1670	  \]
1671	  Post-compose both sides with \((g')_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}\bigr)\).
1672	  Since \((g')_{*}\) is a functor it carries
1673	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp})^{\mathrm{inv}} \circ (\mathrm{pullbackComp})^{\mathrm{hom}} = \mathrm{id}\)
1674	  to the identity, so the right-hand side collapses to \(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\), giving the stated
1675	  expansion.
1676	\end{proof}

2526	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)

2543	        lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
2544	  The proof chains the four pieces into which the generator trace has been
2545	  decomposed. By Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read} the
2546	  source \(\Gamma\bigl(g^*(f_*\widetilde M)\bigr)\) is
2547	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) via \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\); by
2548	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} — which rests on
2549	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor} identifying the cone legs, the single
2550	  Mathlib-absent step —

2552	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) via \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\). By the generator
2553	  trace Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace} the conjugated map
2554	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}\) sends
2555	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). By the inverse formula of
2556	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib} this assignment is exactly
2557	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\); equivalently, in the
2558	  cancellation direction \((r' \otimes a) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\) is
2559	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\).
2560	
2561	  Since \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is a Mathlib linear equivalence carrying
2562	  \emph{no} flatness hypothesis, the conjugated map is an isomorphism; being the
2563	  conjugate of \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) by the two dictionary isomorphisms
2564	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}, \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\), it follows that
2565	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is itself an isomorphism. This closes the affine case
2566	  \emph{directly} on global sections, with no separate abstract-mate-versus-concrete-map
2567	  identification at the level of sheaves. (The full equality
2568	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}
2569	  = \operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\) of the intended signature follows from the
2570	  same chain once \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}, \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) are formed; the
2571	  formalized decl records the \(\mathrm{IsIso}\ \Gamma(\alpha)\) corollary, which is
2572	  what the affine close consumes.)
2573	\end{proof}

2639	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:base_change_map_affine_local, lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
2640	  The proof is the composite of the two named obligations, taken \emph{directly on
2641	  global sections}.

2643	  \medskip
2644	  \emph{First reduction (locality on \(S'\)).} By
2645	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
2646	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
2647	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). This is exactly the
2648	  locality reduction Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}: restricting the
2649	  cartesian square to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R') \subseteq S'\) and
2650	  a chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), the
2651	  hypothesis \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to
2652	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict
2653	  to \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that quasi-coherence is
2654	  used: it is what licenses writing \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\).) Computing the
2655	  fibre product gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\) and
2656	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module
2657	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). The matter is thus reduced to the affine--affine
2658	  model, where the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
2659	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
2660	
2661	  \medskip
2662	  \emph{Passage to global sections.} The functor
2663	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
2664	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful with essential image
2665	  the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently the counit
2666	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
2667	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
2668	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms and, by their naturality,
2669	  \[
2670	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
2671	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
2672	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
2673	  \]
2674	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map in
2675	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). The whole computation therefore stays at the level
2676	  of modules.
2677	
2678	  \medskip
2679	  \emph{Section-level identification (the second obligation).} By the section-level
2680	  computation Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, the map
2681	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\), with its domain \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M}))\) identified with
2682	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) and its codomain \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M})\) identified
2683	  with \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) through the two dictionaries
2684	  Lemmas~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso},
2685	  \emph{is} the canonical cancellation isomorphism
2686	  \[
2687	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
2688	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
2689	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
2690	  \]
2691	  of \(R'\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}). This is the entire
2692	  content of the affine close, and it is performed \emph{directly on the module of
2693	  global sections} — there is no separate abstract adjoint-mate identification at the
2694	  level of sheaves. Because \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with
2695	  no flatness hypothesis whatsoever, \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an isomorphism, hence so is
2696	  \(\alpha\) on the affine chart, and by the locality reduction so is the global
2697	  base-change map. Flatness of \(g\) plays no role in the affine lemma; it enters only
2698	  in the global statement Theorem~\ref{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}, where it is
2699	  used to commute \(- \otimes_A B\) with the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\).
2700	\end{proof}

2702	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
2703	
2704	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
2705	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
2706	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.
2707	
2708	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
2709	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2710	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
2711	  \mathlibok
2712	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
2713	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
2714	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
2715	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
2716	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
2717	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
2718	  \[
2719	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
2720	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2721	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
2722	  \]
2723	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
2724	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
2725	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
2726	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
2727	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
2728	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
2729	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
2730	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
2731	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
2732	\end{lemma}

2734	\begin{theorem}\leanok
2735	[Flat base change, $i=0$ case]
2736	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}
2737	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso}
2738	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
2739	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2740	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change", part for
2741	  % $i = 0$) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
2742	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
2743	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
2744	  % $$
2745	  % \xymatrix{
2746	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
2747	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
2748	  % }
2749	  % $$
2750	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
2751	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
2752	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
2753	  % For any $i \geq 0$
2754	  % \begin{enumerate}
2755	  % \item the base change map of
2756	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
2757	  % is an isomorphism
2758	  % $$
2759	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
2760	  % $$
2761	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
2762	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
2763	  % \end{enumerate}"
2764	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
2765	  \(i = 0\) case.}
2766	  Consider the cartesian square
2767	  \[
2768	    \begin{array}{ccc}
2769	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
2770	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
2771	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
2772	    \end{array}
2773	  \]
2774	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
2775	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
2776	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
2777	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
2778	  \[
2779	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2780	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
2781	  \]
2782	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
2783	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
2784	  \[
2785	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2786	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
2787	  \]
2788	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
2789	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
2790	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
2791	
2792	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
2793	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
2794	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
2795	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
2796	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
2797	  an isomorphism.
2798	\end{theorem}

2865	\begin{proof}
2866	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2867	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
2868	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
2869	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
2870	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
2871	  sequence.
2872	
2873	  \medskip
2874	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
2875	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
2876	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
2877	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
2878	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
2879	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
2880	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
2881	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
2882	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
2883	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
2884	  \[
2885	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
2886	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
2887	  \]
2888	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
2889	
2890	  \medskip
2891	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
2892	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
2893	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
2894	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
2895	  \[
2896	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2897	    \;=\;
2898	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
2899	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
2900	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
2901	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
2902	    \Bigr),
2903	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
2904	  \]
2905	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
2906	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
2907	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
2908	  higher cohomology.
2909	
2910	  \medskip
2911	  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
2912	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
2913	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
2914	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
2915	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
2916	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
2917	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
2918	  \[
2919	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2920	    \qquad
2921	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2922	  \]
2923	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
2924	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
2925	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
2926	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
2927	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}):
2928	  \[
2929	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
2930	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
2931	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2932	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
2933	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
2934	  \]
2935	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
2936	
2937	  \medskip
2938	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
2939	  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
2940	  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
2941	  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
2942	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
2943	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
2944	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
2945	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
2946	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
2947	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
2948	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
2949	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
2950	  \[
2951	    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2952	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
2953	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
2954	  \]
2955	  and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness of \(A \to B\) makes
2956	  \(- \otimes_A B\) preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
2957	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
2958	  \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t - 1\) members), and for
2959	  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
2960	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
2961	\end{proof}

878	  For \(s \in R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), the composite of the inclusion
879	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) (sending \(X_j \mapsto X_j\))
880	  with the isomorphism \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) sends \(s\)
881	  to the constant polynomial \(s \in (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\).
882	\end{lemma}
883	\begin{proof}
884	  Proved directly in Lean by induction on \(s\).
885	\end{proof}

887	\begin{lemma}[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
888	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
889	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
890	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
891	  % NOTE (variable convention): the project's univariate elimination uses
892	  %   MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv, which singles out variable X_0 as the polynomial
893	  %   variable over the coefficient ring MvPolynomial (Fin n) A. The choice of which of
894	  %   the d = n+1 variables is "distinguished/eliminated" is immaterial (a renaming);
895	  %   the informal prose elsewhere says "last variable X_d" but the Lean uses X_0.
896	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
897	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
898	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
899	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
900	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
901	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
902	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
903	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
904	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g; map := MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A A_g).
905	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
906	  %       (F : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A) (hF : F ≠ 0) :
907	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
908	  %         (e : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A ≃ₐ[A] MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A),
909	  %         IsUnit
910	  %           (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv (Localization.Away g) n
911	  %             (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A (Localization.Away g)) (e F))).leadingCoeff
912	  %   The automorphism e is the triangular Nagata substitution; g is the (non-zero)
913	  %   leading coefficient that becomes a unit after inversion.
914	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
915	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
916	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
917	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
918	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
919	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
920	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
921	\end{lemma}
922	
923	\begin{proof}
924	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
925	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
926	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
927	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
928	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
929	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
930	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
931	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
932	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
933	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
934	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
935	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
936	\end{proof}

938	\begin{lemma}[L5b engine anchor — quotient by a monic univariate polynomial is finite]
939	  \label{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite}

943	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(f \in S[X]\) a monic polynomial. Then the quotient
944	  \(S[X]/(f)\) is a module-finite \(S\)-module (indeed free of rank \(\deg f\)).

971	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (matches the landed decl verbatim).

974	  %   (constant inclusion X_j ↦ X_{j+1} = MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ, then

976	  %     (R : Type*) [CommRing R] (n : ℕ)
977	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R)
978	  %       (hp : IsUnit (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p).leadingCoeff) :
979	  %       ((Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {p})).comp
980	  %           (MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ).toRingHom :

982	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (single-variable elimination).}
983	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and \(p \in R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) a
984	  polynomial whose image under the isomorphism
985	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) has a \emph{unit} leading
986	  coefficient — i.e.\ \(p\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to a unit. Then the
987	  quotient \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
988	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) (embedded by \(X_j \mapsto X_j\), \(j = 1, \dots, n\)).
989	\end{lemma}

991	\begin{proof}
992	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
993	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (single-variable elimination).}
994	  Write \(S := R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) and let
995	  \(\Phi : R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S[X_0]\) be the standard \(R\)-algebra
996	  isomorphism, which sends \(X_0\) to the univariate variable and embeds \(S\) as the constants. Put \(q := \Phi(p) \in S[X_0]\); by hypothesis its leading
997	  coefficient \(u\) is a unit, so the associate \(\tilde q := u^{-1} \cdot q\) is monic and
998	  \((\tilde q) = (q)\) in \(S[X_0]\). The isomorphism \(\Phi\) carries the ideal \((p)\) onto
999	  \((q) = (\tilde q)\), hence descends to a ring isomorphism
1000	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p) \cong S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) that is \(S\)-linear, because \(S\) sits
1001	  inside both sides as the constants (respectively as the coefficient subring
1002	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)). By \cref{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite} the quotient
1003	  \(S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) is module-finite over \(S\); transporting finiteness along the
1004	  \(S\)-linear isomorphism shows \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over
1005	  \(S = R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
1006	\end{proof}

1049	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
1050	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
1051	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
1052	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
1053	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1054	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
1055	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
1056	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
1057	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).

1059	
1060	\begin{proof}
1061	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
1062	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
1063	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
1064	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1065	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1066	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
1067	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
1068	
1069	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
1070	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
1071	
1072	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
1073	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
1074	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
1075	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
1076	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
1077	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
1078	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
1079	
1080	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
1081	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
1082	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
1083	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
1084	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
1085	  \(A_g\).
1086	
1087	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
1088	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
1089	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
1090	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
1091	
1092	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
1093	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
1094	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
1095	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
1096	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
1097	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
1098	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
1099	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
1100	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
1101	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
1102	
1103	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
1104	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
1105	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
1106	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
1107	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
1108	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
1109	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
1110	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
1111	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
1112	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
1113	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
1114	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
1115	
1116	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
1117	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
1118	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
1119	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
1120	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
1121	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
1122	
1123	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
1124	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
1125	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
1126	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
1127	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
1128	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
1129	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
1130	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
1131	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
1132	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
1133	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
1134	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
1135	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
1136	
1137	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
1138	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
1139	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1140	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1141	\end{proof}
1142	
1143	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
1144	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
1145	
1146	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1147	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1148	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1149	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1150	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
1151	
1152	\begin{lemma}[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1153	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1154	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1155	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1156	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1157	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1158	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1159	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1160	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1161	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1162	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1163	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1164	\end{lemma}
1165	\begin{proof}
1166	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1167	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1168	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1169	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1170	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1171	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1172	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1173	\end{proof}
1174	
1175	\begin{lemma}[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1176	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1177	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1178	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1179	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1180	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1181	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1182	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1183	\end{lemma}
1184	\begin{proof}
1185	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1186	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1187	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1188	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1189	\end{proof}
1190	
1191	\begin{lemma}[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1192	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1194	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1195	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1196	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1197	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1198	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1199	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1200	\end{lemma}
1201	\begin{proof}
1202	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1203	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1204	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1205	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1206	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1207	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1208	  these conditions gives the claim.
1209	\end{proof}
1210	
1211	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1212	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1213	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1214	  \mathlibok
1215	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1216	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1217	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1218	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1219	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1220	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1221	\end{lemma}

1223	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1224	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1225	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1226	  \mathlibok
1227	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1228	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1229	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1230	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1231	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).
1232	\end{lemma}

1234	\begin{lemma}[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1235	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1236	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1237	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1238	  % LEAN DEPS: the localisation-transport primitives the Lean proof consumes are
1239	  %   IsLocalization.surj                          (clear the denominator of h over A_g)
1240	  %   IsLocalization.map_units                     (the cleared denominator s ∈ powers g is a unit)
1241	  %   IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated        (key ring identification: Away(g·a) on A_h)
1242	  %   IsLocalization.algEquiv                      (ring iso Away(g·a) ≅ A_h)
1243	  %   Module.Free.of_ringEquiv                     (transport freeness across that ring iso)
1244	  %   IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv                (the σ-semilinear module equivalence)
1245	  %   LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization    (upgrade the R-linear equiv to Away(g·a)-linear)
1246	  % The one primitive NOT packaged by Mathlib — that the composite localisation map
1247	  %   ψ := mkLinearMap(powers h) ∘ mkLinearMap(powers g) : T → (T_g)_h
1248	  % satisfies IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ — is proved directly from the three
1249	  % IsLocalizedModule axioms (units act invertibly, surjectivity up to denominators, the
1250	  % equality criterion); there is no packaged localisation-of-localisation lemma to invoke.
1251	  % NOTE: the closed Lean proof DID find a packaged route — the composite-localisation
1252	  % `IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ` step goes through `IsBaseChange.comp` via
1253	  % `isLocalizedModule_iff_isBaseChange`, so the "no packaged lemma" remark above is superseded.
1254	  % WITNESS: the conclusion holds at the SINGLE product f := g·a (not at a square f²).
1255	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1256	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1257	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1258	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1259	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1260	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1261	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1262	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1263	\end{lemma}
1264	
1265	\begin{proof}
1266	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1267	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1268	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1269	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1270	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1271	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1272	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1273	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1274	
1275	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1276	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1277	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1278	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1279	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1280	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1281	
1282	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1283	  \[
1284	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1285	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1286	  \]
1287	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1288	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1289	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1290	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1291	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1292	
1293	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1294	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1295	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1296	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1297	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1298	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1299	\end{proof}

1333	\begin{proof}
1334	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1335	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1336	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1337	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1338	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1339	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE: the induction must generalise the base domain A
1340	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1341	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1342	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1343	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
1344	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
1345	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
1346	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
1347	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
1348	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1349	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1350	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1351	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1352	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1353	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1354	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1355	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1356	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1357	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1358	
1359	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1360	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1361	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1362	
1363	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1364	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1365	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1366	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1367	  \begin{enumerate}
1368	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1369	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1370	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1371	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1372	      \(P_d\)-modules
1373	      \[
1374	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1375	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1376	      \]
1377	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1378	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1379	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1380	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1381	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1382	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1383	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1384	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1385	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1386	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1387	      to \(A_g\).
1388	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1389	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1390	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1391	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1392	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1393	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1394	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1395	      over \(A_f\).
1396	      % NOTE: the tower-descent helper lem:gf_away_tower_descent
1397	      % (free_localizationAway_of_away_tower) is now CLOSED and axiom-clean. The L5 sorry that
1398	      % remains is NOT a missing math step — it is a Lean elaboration blocker: the
1399	      % `OreLocalization` instance *presentations* on the localised quotient `(N ⧸ range φ)_g`
1400	      % (CommSemiring / AddCommMonoid / Module-SMul) emitted by `gf_torsion_reindex` are defeq
1401	      % but not instance-transparent-equal to the presentations the helper input expects. The
1402	      % clean fix is non-local: make `gf_torsion_reindex` emit its conclusion over the canonical
1403	      % `OreLocalization.*` instances (its final `exact` / existential binders), after which the
1404	      % recorded five-line assembly closes verbatim. Do NOT re-dispatch a raw L5 prover round
1405	      % without that instance-alignment refactor first.
1406	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1407	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1408	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1409	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1410	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1411	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1412	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1413	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1414	      completes the induction.
1415	  \end{enumerate}
1416	\end{proof}

1418	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
1419	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
1420	
1421	The following two lemmas package the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms
1422	(flatness and module-finiteness) used by the dévissage chain.
1423	
1424	\begin{lemma}
1425	
1426	
1427	[Flat localization away, finite module]

1462	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1463	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1464	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1465	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1466	  % NOTE (re-signed): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
1467	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
1468	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any
1469	  % non-empty open). The decl (not in archon-protected.yaml) was re-signed to carry
1470	  % the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is the source of truth for the exact
1471	  % binders and the full flatness conclusion.
1472	  %
1473	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
1474	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
1475	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
1476	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
1477	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
1478	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
1479	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
1480	  %
1481	  % LEAN SIGNATURE HEADER (now landed in the .lean file):
1482	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
1483	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] (F : X.Modules)
1484	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
1485	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
1486	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
1487	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
1488	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
1489	
1490	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1491	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1492	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1493	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1494	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1495	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1496	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1497	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1498	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1499	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1500	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1501	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1502	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1503	
1504	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1505	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1506	\end{theorem}
1507	
1508	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1509	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1510	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1511	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1512	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1513	% by restricting to a non-empty
1514	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1515	\begin{proof}
1516	  
1517	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1518	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1519	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1520	
1521	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
1522	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
1523	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
1524	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
1525	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1526	
1527	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1528	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1529	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-type hypothesis makes \(M_j\)
1530	  a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is
1531	  the data fed to the algebraic form.
1532	
1533	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1534	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1535	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1536	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1537	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1538	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1539	
1540	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1541	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the fibrewise flatness conclusion then follows from
1542	  these local freeness statements by the criterion that a module flat at every maximal
1543	  ideal is flat. This produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1544	\end{proof}

1034	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
1035	
1036	\begin{definition}\leanok
1037	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
1038	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
1039	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
1040	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum}
1041	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.hilb}
1042	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1043	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
1044	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
1045	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
1046	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
1047	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
1048	  \begin{itemize}
1049	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
1050	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
1051	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
1052	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
1053	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
1054	    \item a finiteness condition: the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a finite module over the
1055	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
1056	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),
1057	      \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N'\) of \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}).
1058	  \end{itemize}
1059	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
1060	  Hilbert function} is
1061	  \[
1062	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1063	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
1064	  \]
1065	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
1066	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
1067	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
1068	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
1069	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
1070	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
1071	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
1072	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
1073	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
1074	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
1075	\end{definition}

1077	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
1078	
1079	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
1080	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
1081	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
1082	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
1083	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
1084	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1085	
1086	\begin{definition}\leanok
1087	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1088	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1089	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1090	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1091	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1092	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1093	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1094	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1095	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1096	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1097	\end{definition}
1098	
1099	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1100	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1101	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1102	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1103	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1104	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1105	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1106	\end{lemma}
1107	
1108	\begin{proof}
1109	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1110	  \leanok
1111	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1112	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1113	\end{proof}
1114	
1115	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1116	[Degree-shift commutation]
1117	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1119	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1120	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1121	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1122	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1123	  \[
1124	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1125	  \]
1126	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1127	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1128	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1129	\end{lemma}
1130	
1131	\begin{proof}
1132	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1133	  \leanok
1134	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1135	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1136	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1137	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1138	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1139	\end{proof}

1141	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1142	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1143	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1145	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1146	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1147	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1148	\end{lemma}
1149	
1150	\begin{proof}
1151	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1152	  \leanok
1153	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1154	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1155	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1156	\end{proof}

1158	\begin{lemma}\leanok

1160	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1161	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1162	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}

1170	  \leanok

1173	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)

1178	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1179	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
