1193	    \leanok
1194	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1195	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1196	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1197	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1198	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1199	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1200	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1201	  homogeneous.
1202	\end{proof}
1203	
1204	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1205	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1206	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1207	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1208	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1209	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1210	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1211	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1212	  this is recorded here.
1213	\end{lemma}
1214	
1215	\begin{proof}
1216	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1217	  \leanok
1218	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1219	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1220	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1221	\end{proof}
1222	
1223	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1224	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1225	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1226	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1227	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1228	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1229	\end{lemma}
1230	
1231	\begin{proof}
1232	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1233	  \leanok
1234	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1235	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1236	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1237	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1238	\end{proof}
1239	
1240	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1241	[Ambient image identity]
1242	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1244	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1245	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1246	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1247	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1248	  \[
1249	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1250	  \]
1251	\end{lemma}
1252	
1253	\begin{proof}
1254	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1255	  \leanok
1256	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1257	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1258	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1259	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1260	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1261	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1262	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1263	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1264	\end{proof}
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1267	[Ambient distributive law]
1268	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1269	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1270	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1271	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1272	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1273	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1274	  \[
1275	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1276	  \]
1277	\end{lemma}
1278	
1279	\begin{proof}
1280	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1281	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1282	    \leanok
1283	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1284	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1285	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1286	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1287	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1288	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1289	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1290	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1291	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1292	\end{proof}
1293	
1294	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1295	
1296	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1297	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1298	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1299	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1300	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1301	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1302	
1303	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1304	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1305	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1306	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1307	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1308	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1309	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1310	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1311	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1312	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1313	\end{lemma}
1314	
1315	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1316	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1317	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1318	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1319	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1320	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1321	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1322	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1323	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1324	\end{lemma}
1325	
1326	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1327	[The kernel pair nests]
1328	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1329	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1330	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1331	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1332	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1333	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1334	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1335	\end{lemma}
1336	
1337	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1338	[The cokernel pair nests]
1339	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1340	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1341	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1342	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1343	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1344	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1345	\end{lemma}
1346	
1347	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1348	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1349	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1350	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1351	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1352	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1353	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1354	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1355	\end{lemma}
1356	
1357	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1358	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1359	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1360	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1361	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1362	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1363	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1364	\end{lemma}
1365	
1366	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1367	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1368	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1370	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1371	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1372	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1373	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1374	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1375	\end{lemma}
1376	
1377	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1378	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1379	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1381	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1382	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1383	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1384	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1385	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1386	\end{lemma}
1387	
1388	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1389	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1390	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1391	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1392	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1393	  % length-(r-1) datum.
1394	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1395	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1396	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1397	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1398	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1399	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1400	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1401	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1402	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1403	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1404	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1405	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1406	  \[
1407	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1408	    \qquad
1409	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1410	  \]
1411	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1412	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1413	  \begin{enumerate}
1414	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1415	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1416	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1417	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1418	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1419	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1420	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1421	  \end{enumerate}
1422	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1423	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1424	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1425	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1426	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1427	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1428	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1429	\end{lemma}
1430	
1431	\begin{proof}
1432	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1433	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1434	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1435	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1436	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1437	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1438	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1439	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1440	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1441	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1442	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1443	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1444	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1445	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1446	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1447	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1448	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1449	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1450	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1451	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1452	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1453	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1454	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1455	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1456	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1457	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1458	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1459	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1460	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1461	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1462	
1463	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1464	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in

1022	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
1023	  \]
1024	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
1025	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
1026	  \[
1027	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
1028	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
1029	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
1030	  \]
1031	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
1032	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
1033	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
1034	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
1035	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
1036	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
1037	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
1038	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
1039	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
1040	\end{proof}

1042	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
1043	\label{sec:gr_glued}
1044	
1045	\begin{definition}
1046	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1047	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
1048	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
1049	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1050	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
1051	  % affine patches" (read from
1052	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
1053	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1054	  % "Therefore the schemes
1055	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
1056	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
1057	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
1058	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
1059	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
1060	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
1061	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
1062	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1063	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
1064	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
1065	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
1066	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
1067	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
1068	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
1069	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
1070	  \[
1071	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
1072	  \]
1073	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
1074	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
1075	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
1076	
1077	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1078	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1079	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1080	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1081	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1082	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1083	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1084	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1085	  follows.
1086	\end{definition}
1087	
1088	\section{Separatedness}
1089	\label{sec:gr_separated}
1090	
1091	\begin{lemma}
1092	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1093	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
1094	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
1095	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1096	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
1097	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
1098	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1099	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
1100	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
1101	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
1102	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1103	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1104	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1105	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
1106	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
1107	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
1108	\end{lemma}
1109	
1110	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
1111	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
1112	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1113	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1114	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
1115	\begin{proof}
1116	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1117	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
1118	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
1119	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
1120	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
1121	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
1122	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
1123	  homomorphism is surjective.
1124	
1125	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
1126	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
1127	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
1128	  \[
1129	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
1130	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
1131	    \qquad
1132	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1133	  \]
1134	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
1135	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
1136	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
1137	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
1138	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
1139	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
1140	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
1141	  and in particular
1142	  \[
1143	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
1144	  \]
1145	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
1146	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
1147	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
1148	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
1149	  \[
1150	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
1151	  \]
1152	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
1153	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
1154	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
1155	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
1156	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
1157	\end{proof}
1158	
1159	\section{Properness}
1160	\label{sec:gr_proper}
1161	
1162	\begin{lemma}
1163	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1164	  \label{lem:gr_proper}
1165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}
1166	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}
1167	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
1168	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891).
1169	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1170	  % "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
1171	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
1172	  %  Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
1173	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by
1174	  %  a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed
1175	  %  one such $I$, next choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum,
1176	  %  where $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes
1177	  %  the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
1178	  %  $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
1179	  %  $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
1180	  %  lies in $GL_d(\K)$.
1181	  %
1182	  %  Now consider the homomorphism
1183	  %  $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1184	  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
1185	  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
1186	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
1187	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
1188	  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
1189	  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
1190	  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
1191	  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
1192	  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
1193	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
1194	  %  separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that
1195	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
1196	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1197	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1198	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is proper.
1199	\end{lemma}
1200	
1201	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is
1202	% proper. It is enough to verify the valuative criterion of properness for
1203	% discrete valuation rings. Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
1204	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by a ring
1205	% homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed one such $I$, next
1206	% choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum, where
1207	% $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes the discrete valuation. As
1208	% $P^I_I = 1$, note that $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$
1209	% and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$ lies in $GL_d(\K)$. Now consider the homomorphism
1210	% $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1211	% $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$ Then $g$ defines the same morphism
1212	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$, and moreover all $d\times d$ minors
1213	% $X^J_K$ satisfy $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it
1214	% follows from the above that in fact $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of
1215	% $X^J$. Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ factors uniquely via
1216	% $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of schemes
1217	% $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
1218	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked separatedness of
1219	% $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
1220	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891.)
1221	\begin{proof}
1222	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
1223	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
1224	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
1225	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
1226	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
1227	  \[
1228	    \begin{array}{ccc}
1229	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
1230	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
1231	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
1232	    \end{array}
1233	  \]
1234	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
1235	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
1236	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1237	
1238	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
1239	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
1240	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
1241	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
1242	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
1243	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
1244	  \(K\). Define
1245	  \[
1246	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
1247	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
1248	  \]
1249	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
1250	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
1251	  landing in \(U^J\).
1252	
1253	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
1254	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
1255	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
1256	  \[
1257	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
1258	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
1259	    \qquad
1260	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
1261	  \]
1262	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
1263	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
1264	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
1265	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
1266	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
1267	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
1268	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
1269	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
1270	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
1271	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
1272	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
1273	
1274	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
1275	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
1276	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1277	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
1278	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
1279	  proper.
1280	\end{proof}
1281	
1282	\section{Out of scope}
1283	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
1284	
1285	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
1286	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
1287	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
1288	are not treated here:
1289	\begin{itemize}
1290	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
1291	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
1292	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
1293	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1294	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
1295	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
1296	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
1297	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
1298	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
1299	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
1300	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
1301	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
1302	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
1303	    that base change.
1304	\end{itemize}

1595	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1596	
1597	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1598	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1599	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1600	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1601	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1602	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1603	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1604	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1605	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1606	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1607	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).

1610	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1611	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1612	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1613	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}

1615	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1616	  ring homomorphism}

1619	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)

1622	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1623	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1624	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1625	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1626	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
